This distractor misrepresents a speculative recommendation as a derived inference, making it a strong example of a speculation-based distractor.

224.Option: The lack of significant difference in CD8 T cell activation after multivariable adjustment implies that W. bancrofti infection does not influence systemic immune activation in the study population.
224.Discussion_Source: Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD8 T cell were increased in subjects with W. bancrofti (mean 29.5%) infection compared to subjects without filarial infection (mean 20.8%). However, this difference was not significant after adjusting for study site and other confounding factors in the multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S2).
Evidence: This difference was not significant after adjusting for study site and other confounding factors in the multivariable analysis.

The association between increased frequencies of activated HLA-DRpos and HLA-DRpos/CD38pos and effector memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells in peripheral blood and W. bancrofti infection was independent of other host and environmental factors, suggesting a causal link between these two factors.
224.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the statistical result by equating non-significance with biological irrelevance. The passage shows that CD8 activation was elevated in univariable analysis but lost significance after adjustment—this does not mean the infection has no effect, only that the effect may be confounded or not robust. The correct inference focuses on CD4 T cells, which showed consistent, significant, and adjusted associations. This distractor reverses the interpretation of statistical significance and ignores the distinction between CD4 and CD8 responses, making it a causal reversal with a subtle but critical flaw.

225.Option: The presence of diarrhea in dengue infection may be related to plasma leakage that causes malabsorption, suggesting it could serve as an early predictor of severe dengue disease.
225.Discussion_Source: This study, to the best of our knowledge, is the first study suggesting that diarrhea may be a predictor for DHF. Its highest prevalence just before the stage of shock suggests that it may be related to plasma leakage that may cause malabsorption. Further study to clearly define the association between diarrhea and DHF and its mechanism is warranted.
Evidence: The prevalence of most symptoms was highest during the first 2 days of illness and then slowly declined.

In contrast, in severe dengue infection (i.e. DHF), the prevalence of anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain and diarrhea during day 3–5 of illness was found to increase instead of decrease and it will indicate the risk for more severe disease.
The study found that diarrhea was more common in DHF compared to DF and UF.
225.Rationale: While the Discussion speculates that diarrhea may be linked to plasma leakage and could predict DHF, this is presented as a hypothesis requiring further study. The Passage Body does not establish a causal or predictive relationship between diarrhea and disease severity. The question asks about clinical recognition of dengue, not about pathophysiological mechanisms or future research directions. Thus, this conclusion cannot be derived from the passage alone and constitutes a speculative distractor.

225.Option: The high proportion of undifferentiated fever (UF) cases in this cohort suggests that dengue infection is more commonly asymptomatic than previously believed.
225.Discussion_Source: The high incidence of UF cases that did not meet the clinical criteria for DF or DHF was not anticipated in the study design.
Evidence: There were 156 cases (49.8%) of UF, 123 cases (39.3%) of DF, and 34 cases (10.9%) of DHF.
Among 41 cases with primary antibody responses, 26 cases had UF (63.4%), 14 cases had DF (34.2%) and 1 case had DHF grade 1 (2.4%).
UF was defined as acute febrile illness with no clinical criteria for DF or DHF but serologically confirmed dengue infection.
225.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets 'undifferentiated fever' (UF) as 'asymptomatic' infection. However, UF is explicitly defined as a symptomatic condition (acute febrile illness) that does not meet clinical criteria for DF or DHF. The passage clearly distinguishes UF from asymptomatic infection, and the Discussion notes that UF cases had clinical symptoms, albeit not meeting standard diagnostic thresholds. This causal reversal of symptom status makes the conclusion invalid and a strong distractor.

225.Option: The WHO's new guidelines for dengue diagnosis may fail to identify UF cases because clinicians are unlikely to suspect dengue in patients presenting with mild, non-specific symptoms.
225.Discussion_Source: It is stated in the WHO's new guideline for dengue diagnosis [19] that the presence of abdominal pain or tenderness, persistent vomiting, clinical fluid accumulation, mucosal bleed, lethargy, restlessness, liver enlargement are clinical warning signs of severe dengue disease. The finding in this study that DHF cases had higher prevalence of nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain supports this guideline. On the other hand, we found that the presence of lethargy or drowsiness in the first few days of illness may not necessarily indicate severe dengue infection. The significance of drowsiness/lethargy needs to be further defined.
Evidence: The high incidence of UF cases that did not meet the clinical criteria for DF or DHF was not anticipated in the study design.
UF cases were often diagnosed as acute febrile illness/suspected viral infection.
The study found that drowsiness/lethargy was more common in UF during day 2–5 of illness.
225.Rationale: This statement is plausible and appears in the Discussion, but it relies on external knowledge of WHO guidelines (cited as [19]) not described in the Passage Body. The passage does not state that clinicians fail to suspect dengue in mild cases; it only reports that UF cases were not anticipated and were diagnosed as viral infections. The inference about guideline failure requires information beyond the passage, making this an external dependency distractor.

225.Option: The clinical manifestations of dengue infection are highly variable across different serotypes, with DEN4 being associated with more severe disease despite its lower prevalence.
225.Discussion_Source: This study also found that DEN4 did not cause severe disease. However, we could not show the statistically significant association between specific virus serotypes and disease severity.
Evidence: DEN4 was found to cause only UF, DF and DHF grade 1 and seemed to have less headache, anorexia, nausea/vomiting and rash compared to other DEN serotypes.
There was no significant association among infecting dengue serotypes and types of antibody response.
The study found that DEN4 did not cause severe disease.
225.Rationale: This distractor reverses the actual finding: the Discussion explicitly states that DEN4 did not cause severe disease and may even be associated with milder symptoms. The conclusion that DEN4 causes more severe disease contradicts the evidence. This causal reversal is subtle and plausible, as it misrepresents the direction of the association between serotype and severity, making it a strong distractor that tests careful reading.

226.Option: The high prevalence of tungiasis in dogs and their frequent movement between households and villages suggest that they are the most important reservoirs for the spread of T. penetrans in rural Uganda.
226.Discussion_Source: Although the prevalence of tungiasis in dogs was the second highest after that of pigs, dogs were considerably less frequently infected than in previous studies in Nigeria and Brazil [15–17]. Therefore, dogs appear to be less important as animal reservoirs in Uganda than in Nigeria and Brazil. Moreover, the number of dogs in Ugandan rural villages is comparatively small. The significance of dogs in the epidemiology of T. penetrans infections in the Ugandan setting generally decreased with dog age probably because of the increasing thickness of the keratin layer of the foot pads as the dogs mature.
Evidence: Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig); goats median = 20 (6 and 34 lesions in 2 goats); dogs median = 2 (inter quartile range = 2–3; range = 1–8).
The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).

In 40 (17%, 95% CI 12.7–22.3) of the sampled households, animal and human tungiasis coexisted. These constituted 50% and 64.5% of the households with human and animal tungiasis respectively. Animal tungiasis increased the odds of the occurrence of human tungiasis in households by six times (OR = 6.1, 95% CI 3.3–11.4%; p<0.001) and vice versa.

Pigs were the animal species predominantly affected and they also had the highest intensity of infection. These findings corroborate observations made in Nigeria [17]. Since both, the prevalence of tungiasis in pigs at village and household level as well as the intensity of infection correlated strongly with the respective measurements in humans, it can be proposed that pigs are the reservoir hosts that contribute most to the occurrence of tungiasis in humans in the study area.
226.Rationale: This distractor misrepresents the evidence by elevating dogs to a primary reservoir role despite the passage showing they have low prevalence, low infection intensity, and are less important than pigs in Uganda. The Discussion explicitly states dogs are less important in Uganda than in other regions and that their role is unclear due to low case numbers. The distractor ignores the strong correlation with pigs and instead focuses on speculative movement patterns not supported by the data.

226.Option: The absence of tungiasis in cattle and chickens suggests that these species are not susceptible to T. penetrans infection, and therefore cannot contribute to transmission in the study area.
226.Discussion_Source: Cattle and other mammalian species with the exception of pigs, dogs, cats and goats were not found to carry T. penetrans. However, since only a few of them were sampled, it cannot be excluded that they also act as reservoirs of T. penetrans in the study area and other areas of Uganda. Reports from South America indicate that cattle seem to be particularly susceptible to T. trimamillata infection and T. penetrans infection is encountered usually as a co-infection [3]. In the study area, the absence of tungiasis among cattle could also be attributable to the higher rate of ectoparasite control in cattle (46% of the cattle owning households) than for other species.
Evidence: Cattle and other mammalian species with the exception of pigs, dogs, cats and goats were not found to carry T. penetrans.
Since only a few of them were sampled, it cannot be excluded that they also act as reservoirs of T. penetrans in the study area and other areas of Uganda.
The absence of tungiasis among cattle could also be attributable to the higher rate of ectoparasite control in cattle (46% of the cattle owning households) than for other species.
226.Rationale: This distractor draws a definitive conclusion about non-susceptibility based on negative findings in a small sample, which contradicts the Discussion's explicit caution that absence of detection does not imply absence of risk. The passage acknowledges that cattle may be susceptible and that control practices may have influenced the results. The distractor fails to account for sampling limitations and confounding factors like ectoparasite control.

226.Option: The strong correlation between human and animal tungiasis is likely due to shared environmental exposure rather than direct zoonotic transmission, indicating that pigs play a minimal role in the transmission cycle.
226.Discussion_Source: The patterns of human and animal infections were similar in the study area among animal rearing households i.e. the prevalence and infection intensity strongly correlated. This, together with their geographical coexistence (Fig 1), suggests an inter-linkage between animal and human T. penetrans infections in Uganda. This however requires validation by systematically sampling humans. Since the focus of the present study was on identification of important reservoir hosts of zoonotic tungiasis, only animal rearing households were included. Also co-existence of infections in both humans and animals may indicate a common source of infection which was not directly examined in this study. However, occurrence of tungiasis in animals increases environmental contamination which in turn may increase the prevalence and intensity of infections in humans and vice-versa resulting in the strong correlation.
Evidence: The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).
The median number of lesion in children 3–8 years old on one randomly selected foot was plotted against the median number of lesions in animals from the same household (rho = 0.47, p<0.001).

In 40 (17%, 95% CI 12.7–22.3) of the sampled households, animal and human tungiasis coexisted. These constituted 50% and 64.5% of the households with human and animal tungiasis respectively. Animal tungiasis increased the odds of the occurrence of human tungiasis in households by six times (OR = 6.1, 95% CI 3.3–11.4%; p<0.001) and vice versa.

Pigs were the animal species predominantly affected and they also had the highest intensity of infection. These findings corroborate observations made in Nigeria [17]. Since both, the prevalence of tungiasis in pigs at village and household level as well as the intensity of infection correlated strongly with the respective measurements in humans, it can be proposed that pigs are the reservoir hosts that contribute most to the occurrence of tungiasis in humans in the study area.
226.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the causal direction by attributing the correlation to a shared environmental source while downplaying the role of pigs. The Discussion acknowledges a common source is possible but emphasizes that animal infections increase environmental contamination, which supports a zoonotic transmission pathway. The high parasite load in pigs and the strong statistical associations with human infection levels directly support pigs as a key reservoir, not a passive indicator of environmental exposure.

226.Option: The fact that pigs are not confined during the crop-growing season suggests that their role in transmission is limited to the dry season, when sand flea activity peaks.
226.Discussion_Source: Pigs are mostly reared on earthen floors with minimal environmental sanitation management. For this reason, dirty pig dwellings were identified as a risk factor for pig tungiasis at least in the bivariate analysis. The organic material may favor the off-host development of T. penetrans thus predisposing pigs to heavy infections [20]. This was in fact indicated by the high proportion of viable lesions particularly in pigs as compared to dogs. Of course, the practice of allowing them to roam on human compounds also increases the risk of their infection and also facilitates the shedding of sand flea eggs in human dwellings when pigs are infected.
Evidence: The study was conducted between January 22 and March 28, 2014 which coincided with the middle and end of the dry season when the attack rate of T. penetrans usually peaks [20].
Pigs were confined on earthen floors during the crop growing season and released to scavenge after harvest.

The prevalence of tungiasis and the infection intensity among pigs reported in this study in some villages were comparable to those reported in Nigeria [17] but were much higher than those in Brazil [25].
Pigs were the animal species predominantly affected and they also had the highest intensity of infection.
226.Rationale: This distractor incorrectly assumes that pig exposure is limited to the dry season based on their release schedule, but the passage shows pigs are infected year-round due to their constant exposure to contaminated environments. The study was conducted during the dry season, but the high infection rates and parasite loads in pigs are not seasonal artifacts. The Discussion emphasizes that environmental contamination from pigs contributes to transmission throughout the year, not just during peak seasons.

227.Option: Temperature was the most influential factor in determining tsetse presence in the multivariate logistic regression model, with a strong positive association (p<0.05, OR = 2.63).
227.Discussion_Source: Temperature remained highly influential in determining tsetse presence in the multivariate model (p<0.05, OR = 2.63).
Evidence: After accounting for spatial autocorrelation, the covariates temperature, rainfall, and cropland lost their statistical significance in influencing tsetse presence or absence.

The use of a spatial autologistic regression enabled the detection of key environmental variables that are highly influential in positively determining tsetse presence and these were; forests and riverine vegetation.
Tsetse presence was not significantly correlated with temperature (p>0.05).
227.Rationale: This distractor misrepresents the final model outcome by citing unadjusted model results. The question specifically asks about inferences after adjusting for spatial dependencies, but this option relies on the initial multivariate logistic regression, which does not account for spatial autocorrelation. The correct inference requires recognizing that temperature lost significance after adjustment, making this a plausible but invalid conclusion based on incomplete data.

227.Option: Rainfall and cropland were found to be positively associated with tsetse presence in the final autologistic regression model, indicating their importance in supporting tsetse populations.
227.Discussion_Source: Tsetse presence was not correlated with cropland (p>0.05). Tsetse presence was not significantly correlated with rainfall. The entire study area had monthly total rainfall ranging from 34 to 339 mm with no significant spatial variation across the study area. Therefore, it is unlikely that precipitation would influence tsetse distributions.
Evidence: After accounting for spatial autocorrelation, the covariates temperature, rainfall, and cropland lost their statistical significance in influencing tsetse presence or absence.

The use of a spatial autologistic regression enabled the detection of key environmental variables that are highly influential in positively determining tsetse presence and these were; forests and riverine vegetation.
Tsetse presence was not significantly correlated with rainfall (p>0.05).
227.Rationale: This distractor incorrectly claims positive associations for rainfall and cropland in the final model, directly contradicting the passage’s explicit statement that both variables lost significance after adjusting for spatial autocorrelation. It is a plausible-sounding conclusion based on early univariate results but fails to reflect the adjusted model, making it a strong distractor for readers who overlook the distinction between univariate and spatially adjusted findings.

227.Option: The spatial resolution of the land cover data used in the study was insufficient to detect small rivers and streams, which may explain the absence of tsetse predictions along the river Nile.
227.Discussion_Source: The spatial resolution of the land cover data used may not have been detailed enough to enable small rivers to be detected.
Evidence: The predictive models did not predict tsetse presence along the river Nile and this may be due to the spatial resolution of the covariate data used (1 km) not allowing accurate representation of relatively small areas of suitable habitat.
Tsetse presence was positively correlated with riverine landcover.

The use of a spatial autologistic regression enabled the detection of key environmental variables that are highly influential in positively determining tsetse presence and these were; forests and riverine vegetation.
227.Rationale: This statement introduces a speculative explanation (about small rivers) that is not directly supported by the data in the passage. While the discussion notes a possible limitation of spatial resolution, the question asks for an inference about environmental factors influencing tsetse distribution after adjusting for spatial dependencies. This distractor shifts focus to a methodological caveat rather than the actual determinants identified in the final model, making it a plausible but unsupported inference.

227.Option: Savannah vegetation and elevation were positively associated with tsetse presence, as they provide suitable microclimatic conditions for the survival of G. f. fuscipes.
227.Discussion_Source: Savannah vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 0.993) and elevation (p<0.05, OR = 0.997) retained their negative association with tsetse presence.
Evidence: Savannah vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 0.993) and elevation (p<0.05, OR = 0.997) retained their negative association with tsetse presence.
Tsetse presence was negatively correlated with savannah vegetation. Such vegetation can be categorised as “humanised” or “disturbed” landscapes, and tsetse flies usually avoid disturbed habitats.
Tsetse presence was also negatively correlated with elevation.
227.Rationale: This distractor reverses the causal direction of the relationship between environmental variables and tsetse presence. The passage clearly states that both savannah vegetation and elevation are negatively associated with tsetse presence, yet this option claims a positive association. It misrepresents the evidence by reversing the sign of the odds ratio, making it a strong causal reversal distractor that appears plausible to readers who misread or misremember the direction of association.

228.Option: The use of surface water access points (SWAPs) as a mask for environmental variable extraction produced the highest correlation between predicted and observed prevalence values, indicating that SWAPs are the most accurate proxy for schistosomiasis transmission risk.
228.Discussion_Source: According to a visual assessment, environmental data extracted using the SWAP mask more precisely delineated water bodies as high-risk locations within communities (Fig 5). This mask also produced the highest correlation between model predicted and observed prevalence values, depicting heterogeneity in transmission risk among communities (Table 4).
Evidence: The SWAP mask produced the highest correlation between model predicted and observed prevalence values.
Environmental data extracted using the SWAP mask more precisely delineated water bodies as high-risk locations.
The final model combined environmental variables from the SWAP mask with WASH variables, but the SWAP access indicator itself was not important in the final model.
228.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the role of SWAPs by treating the SWAP mask as a direct proxy for transmission risk, when the Discussion clarifies that the mask was used to extract environmental variables, not as a predictor itself. The model results show that SWAP access was not important in the final model, and the high correlation is attributed to better spatial alignment with transmission sites, not to SWAPs being a direct risk factor. This conclusion overreaches the evidence by conflating methodological utility with causal inference.

228.Option: Improved water access, as measured by functional improved water sources (FIWS), is a significant predictor of reduced schistosomiasis risk in the study communities.
228.Discussion_Source: FIWS and SWAP access indicators were not important in the final model.
Evidence: FIWS and SWAP access indicators were not important in the final model.

The final model results confirmed this hypothesis, with groundwater iron content being the predominant schistosomiasis risk factor with a much higher IncNodePurity value as compared to any of the environmental variables (Fig 6).
In our study, variables of improved and unimproved water access were not predictive of schistosomiasis risk, consistently with the findings of Lai et al. [4].
228.Rationale: This distractor contradicts the model results explicitly stated in the Discussion. The correct inference is that improved water access (FIWS) was not a significant predictor, while groundwater iron concentration was. This distractor misrepresents the findings by assuming that access to improved sources reduces risk, which is not supported by the data. It relies on general public health assumptions rather than the specific model outcomes.

228.Option: The high predictive power of the final model, as indicated by R2 and RMSE values, suggests that environmental and WASH variables together can reliably forecast schistosomiasis prevalence at the community level.
228.Discussion_Source: Overall, the models had relatively low predictive power and predicted prevalence values deviated substantially from the observed values, indicating overprediction in the low-prevalence range and underprediction in the high-prevalence range.
Evidence: Overall, the models had relatively low predictive power and predicted prevalence values deviated substantially from the observed values.
The addition of WASH variables only slightly increased the R2 value from 0.15 to 0.17 and decreased the RMSE from 9.47 to 9.03.
This is most likely due to the effects of preventive chemotherapy on prevalence measures.
228.Rationale: This distractor directly contradicts the Discussion’s explicit statement about low predictive power. The model’s R2 and RMSE values indicate poor fit, and the Discussion attributes this to the impact of recent preventive chemotherapy. The distractor misrepresents the model’s performance as strong, when the evidence shows it is weak. It fails to recognize that low R2 and large deviations from observed values undermine reliability, making this a clear case of external dependency on model performance metrics not supported by the passage.

228.Option: Elevated iron concentration in groundwater is a direct cause of schistosomiasis infection, as it increases the viability of Schistosoma cercariae in water bodies.
228.Discussion_Source: Our prior studies have provided qualitative support for the hypothesis that unfavorable groundwater quality in improved water sources (i.e., boreholes and piped water systems) for drinking and laundry is a significant driver of increased surface water use, serving as an indirect risk factor for schistosomiasis transmission.
Evidence: Elevated iron and TDS concentrations in BHs may increase reliance on contaminated surface water bodies, thereby potentially serving as indirect risk factors.

The final model results confirmed this hypothesis, with groundwater iron content being the predominant schistosomiasis risk factor with a much higher IncNodePurity value as compared to any of the environmental variables.
The mechanism is indirect: poor groundwater quality leads to increased surface water use, which increases exposure to cercariae.
228.Rationale: This distractor reverses the causal direction by claiming iron concentration directly causes infection, when the passage clearly frames it as an indirect risk factor. The correct inference is that iron content influences human behavior (increased surface water use), which in turn increases transmission risk. The distractor misrepresents the biological mechanism, turning a behavioral pathway into a direct biological one, which is not supported by the evidence. This is a classic causal reversal that exploits the plausibility of iron’s role in water quality while misinterpreting its epidemiological function.

229.Option: The association between urban residence and leptospirosis seropositivity suggests that urban environments in USVI may have higher levels of environmental contamination with Leptospira spp. due to increased rodent populations and poor infrastructure.
229.Discussion_Source: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
Evidence: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.

This finding makes sense in the context of rodent sightings and exposure being predictive of seropositivity, as contact with rodents and their urine may be more common in urban areas, especially those with poor infrastructure.
229.Rationale: This distractor infers a causal environmental mechanism (higher contamination due to rodents and poor infrastructure) based on a statistically significant bivariate association. However, the Discussion explicitly cautions against interpreting this association as reliable due to subjective classification and lack of significance in multivariate analysis. The passage does not provide direct evidence of environmental contamination levels or rodent density by area, so this causal explanation cannot be derived from the passage alone.

229.Option: Urban residents in USVI are at higher risk of leptospirosis due to greater exposure to floodwater and standing water after hurricanes, which are known to harbor Leptospira spp.
229.Discussion_Source: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
Evidence: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.

The serosurvey was conducted approximately 18 months after the hurricanes, however MAT-positive serology results do not indicate the timing of Leptospira spp. exposure and anti-Leptospira antibodies can persist for years, therefore it is difficult to determine whether seropositive persons were exposed before or after the 2017 hurricanes
229.Rationale: This distractor assumes that urban residents were more exposed to post-hurricane floodwater, which is not supported by the passage. The Discussion notes that the seropositivity finding was not confirmed in multivariate analysis and that exposure timing cannot be determined from serology. The passage does not compare floodwater exposure by urban vs. rural areas, so this causal inference about environmental exposure is speculative and not derivable from the provided evidence.

229.Option: The higher seropositivity among urban residents reflects a true geographic disparity in leptospirosis risk, indicating that urban areas in USVI should be prioritized for targeted public health interventions.
229.Discussion_Source: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
Evidence: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.

No significant difference was detected in the seropositivity of participants between islands; however, this survey was designed to estimate seropositivity across USVI and our sample size did not have adequate statistical power to detect a difference between islands.
229.Rationale: This distractor recommends public health action based on an association that the Discussion explicitly cautions against interpreting as reliable. The correct inference is that the association is not robust due to methodological limitations. The passage does not provide sufficient evidence to justify prioritizing urban areas for interventions, especially since the association was not confirmed in multivariate analysis and the classification was subjective.

229.Option: Urban residents in USVI are more likely to have been exposed to Leptospira spp. through contact with domestic animals such as cows and rodents, which are more prevalent in urban settings.
229.Discussion_Source: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
Evidence: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.

Two types of animal exposures were identified as independently associated with seropositivity by logistic regression, including contact with rodents or their body fluids and/or seeing them around the home or property, and contact with cows or their body fluids.
229.Rationale: This distractor links urban residence to animal exposure (rodents and cows), which is not directly supported by the passage. While rodent and cow contact are independently associated with seropositivity, the passage does not state that these animals are more prevalent in urban areas. The Discussion explicitly warns against relying on the urban residence association due to subjectivity and lack of multivariate confirmation, making this causal explanation unsupported by the evidence.

230.Option: The real-time surveillance system demonstrated in this study relies on a centralized, paper-based reporting framework that is highly efficient and error-prone, as evidenced by the high completion rates and remote monitoring of data.
230.Discussion_Source: In 1998, the African regional office of the World Health Organization (WHO) started promoting the Integrated Disease Surveillance and Response (IDSR) framework for strengthening national public health surveillance capabilities at all levels in Africa [18]. This groundbreaking program was, however, largely paper-based and has been reported to be generally inefficient and error-prone with low completeness and poor timely reporting.
Evidence: The mHealth reporters were asked first to complete and store the surveys locally on their mobile devices in the field, and subsequently, depending on WiFi availability, upload them daily or weekly to a secure web-hosted database.
The accuracy and completeness of the surveys submitted were assessed by a) daily remote data monitoring and b) periodic quality-control visits made to a randomly selected sub-set of households.

With the exception of one reporter (targeting Ludok and Olam villages) who abandoned the program on week 6 due to health-related issues, the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).
230.Rationale: This distractor falsely claims the system is paper-based and efficient, directly contradicting the passage, which explicitly describes a mobile, digital, and real-time system. The Discussion source is used to misrepresent the paper-based IDSR system as the model being evaluated, creating an external dependency. The high completion rate and monitoring processes are real, but they pertain to the mHealth system, not a paper-based one. This distractor misattributes the system type and undermines the correct inference about digital surveillance effectiveness.

230.Option: The high survey completion rate of 97.3% indicates that the mHealth system is fully independent of network connectivity and can operate without any delays in data transmission, even in areas with poor infrastructure.
230.Discussion_Source: The most common technical issues were frequent power cuts, poor network coverage, slow upload speeds and unreliable GPS satellites. Inconsistent connectivity led to difficulties in survey uploading (e.g., some mHealth workers reported traveling to a specific hotspot or even climbing a particular tree to access satisfactory signal strength and network coverage).
Evidence: The mHealth reporters were asked first to complete and store the surveys locally on their mobile devices in the field, and subsequently, depending on WiFi availability, upload them daily or weekly to a secure web-hosted database.

With the exception of one reporter (targeting Ludok and Olam villages) who abandoned the program on week 6 due to health-related issues, the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).
230.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the data by claiming the system operates without delay or dependency on connectivity, despite the passage explicitly describing poor network coverage and upload delays. The high completion rate refers to survey completion, not transmission timeliness. The correct inference acknowledges delays of 1–7 days due to connectivity issues, while this distractor reverses the causal relationship, suggesting the system is immune to infrastructure limitations. This is a causal reversal that misrepresents the operational constraints described in the Discussion.

230.Option: The use of GPS stamps and timestamped data in the Magpi system ensures that all health events are accurately geolocated and temporally precise, enabling perfect spatial and temporal mapping of disease outbreaks in real time.
230.Discussion_Source: After comparing our maps (Fig 3) and the data collected (Table 2), it became readily apparent that many geotracers malfunctioned or, most likely, many remote areas had weak or no coverage of cellular communication, causing limitations in acquiring GPS stamps and compromising the ability to produce maps using GPS coordinates.
Evidence: The image shows a) the user name associated with the mHealth reporter sending the survey record, b) the survey record start and end times, c) the time the record was submitted/uploaded to the Magpi server and d) the GPS stamp.
The Magpi interface also made it possible to visualize NS health-related data over a defined period of time (Fig 3) with the goal to assess fluctuations and carry out temporal interpretations.
230.Rationale: This distractor overstates the reliability of GPS and timestamping, claiming perfect accuracy and real-time mapping. However, the Discussion explicitly states that GPS functionality was compromised in many areas due to weak coverage, limiting map production. The correct inference acknowledges near-real-time monitoring with a 1–7 day delay, not perfect precision. This distractor uses hedging language (

230.Option: The mHealth system's success in data collection and monitoring is primarily due to the high level of formal medical training and clinical expertise of the lay reporters, who were able to accurately diagnose and report neurological conditions without supervision.
230.Discussion_Source: The mHealth reporters were recruited and trained. The training covered basic concepts of health and disease, the purpose and content of the program, clinical and social aspects of NS, use and maintenance of the equipment (cell phones, solar batteries, bicycles), questionnaire presentation, data collection and submission, home and community entry, informed consent administration and patient confidentiality.
Evidence: Eight male and female Ugandan lay mHealth reporters, age 20–30 years, were recruited and trained.
Almost all mHealth reporters had experience using smartphone devices prior to the surveillance feasibility project and were not intimidated by the technology.
The accuracy and completeness of the surveys submitted were assessed by a) daily remote data monitoring and b) periodic quality-control visits made to a randomly selected sub-set of households.
230.Rationale: This distractor falsely attributes the system's effectiveness to clinical expertise and diagnostic ability of lay reporters, which contradicts the passage. The study explicitly states the reporters were 'lay' and not trained as medical professionals. Their role was data collection, not diagnosis. The correct inference focuses on operational feasibility and surveillance capability, not clinical accuracy. This is a causal reversal that misrepresents the reporters' role and overestimates their medical competence, relying on a misinterpretation of the training content.

231.Option: The observed reduction in the number of newly detected leprosy cases after 2004 can be explained by socioeconomic development, improved access to health services, an effective surveillance system, better travel opportunities, and improved education for the population.
231.Discussion_Source: When comparing the number of newly detected leprosy cases before and after the establishment of the special fund for leprosy by the central government, the observed reduction in the number of cases can be explained by socioeconomic development, improved access to health services, an effective surveillance system, better travel opportunities, and improved education for the population.
Evidence: The number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased from 703 in 1990 to 353 in 2003, a reduction of 50% in time period 1.

After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.
231.Rationale: This distractor attributes the decline in leprosy cases to broader socioeconomic factors, which are speculative and not directly supported by the passage. While the Discussion mentions these factors as possible explanations, the question asks for an inference based on the historical data and public health interventions described in the Passage Body. The passage explicitly links the decline to specific interventions (MDT, special fund, surveillance system), not general societal improvements. Thus, this conclusion cannot be derived from the passage alone and represents an external dependency.

231.Option: The increase in early detection rates from 53.9% to 60.3% between time periods 1 and 2 suggests that contact examination and health education have been the primary drivers of the reduction in leprosy burden in Yunnan.
231.Discussion_Source: Early detection has increased over the years and may have contributed to the observed slight reduction in the proportion of pediatric cases and patients with MB leprosy and G2D.
Evidence: The percentage of newly detected patients belonging to an ethnic minority group increased from 51.4% (n = 3533) in time period 1 to 68.7% (n = 4181) in time period 2 (P <0.01).
The percentage of detected cases involving G2D decreased slightly from 22.6% (n = 1550) in time period 1 to 19.2% (n = 812) in time period 2 (P <0.01).
The number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased from 6,868 in time period 1 to 4,184 in time period 2 (P<0.01).
231.Rationale: This distractor misattributes causality by identifying contact examination and health education as the 'primary drivers' of the overall decline. While the passage notes that early detection improved and that these strategies were part of the intervention package, it does not provide evidence to isolate them as the main cause. The correct inference requires integrating multiple interventions, not singling out one. This distractor overemphasizes a single component and introduces a causal claim not supported by the data, making it a plausible but invalid inference.

231.Option: The continued presence of leprosy in 17 highly endemic counties indicates that multidrug treatment (MDT) has been ineffective in preventing transmission in remote, mountainous regions of Yunnan.
231.Discussion_Source: The 17 highly endemic counties are heterogeneously distributed throughout the province. Understanding which geographical areas require intervention is fundamental for cost-effective disease control, and spatial analysis can indicate priority areas for local control programs [14,15]. Overcrowding, lower socioeconomic indicators, a lack of access to health services and precarious sanitation are underlying characteristics that impact the incidence of leprosy, indicating that poverty increases the likelihood of transmission of M. leprae [15].
Evidence: Since 1990, MDT has been used to treat leprosy in every county in Yunnan.
By the end of 2019, 85.5% (100/117) of the leprosy-endemic counties had achieved the leprosy elimination target (Fig 3).
Currently, 17/117 (14.5%) of the counties remain leprosy-endemic regions.
231.Rationale: This distractor reverses the causal interpretation by implying that MDT failed in remote areas, which contradicts the passage’s evidence that MDT was implemented province-wide and led to a substantial decline in cases and endemic areas. The passage attributes the persistence in 17 counties to structural challenges (poverty, access, sanitation), not treatment failure. This causal reversal misrepresents the effectiveness of MDT and cannot be derived from the passage, making it a strong distractor based on misinterpretation of evidence.

231.Option: The high proportion of multibacillary (MB) leprosy cases (up to 74.3% in 2019) suggests that the current leprosy elimination strategies in Yunnan are failing to prevent the progression of early-stage disease to advanced forms.
231.Discussion_Source: The percentage of multibacillary (MB) cases was relatively high in most of the years investigated, ranging from 59.0% in 2008 to 74.3% in 2019 (Table 1).
Evidence: The number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased from 703 in 1990 to 353 in 2003, a reduction of 50% in time period 1.

After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).
The percentage of detected cases involving G2D decreased slightly from 22.6% (n = 1550) in time period 1 to 19.2% (n = 812) in time period 2 (P <0.01).
231.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the high MB rate as a sign of failure, but the passage shows that despite high MB proportions, key outcomes like G2D and new case detection are declining. The correct inference is that the strategies are reducing overall burden, including advanced disease. This distractor ignores the trend data and focuses on a single static statistic, creating a false impression of failure. It is a plausible-sounding but invalid inference based on selective interpretation of data.

232.Option: The failure to diagnose rabies in clinical settings, combined with a lack of clinical awareness among health professionals, contributes to the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
232.Discussion_Source: In other countries in sub-Saharan Africa, a lack of clinical awareness of rabies causes many cases to be misdiagnosed as non-rabies meningo-encephalitis [11–14]. A more regular training program directed to health professionals and incorporation of rabies into under graduate training curricula would avoid many misdiagnoses.
Evidence: None of the 14 cases of human rabies detected were notified through the routine surveillance system.
All cases were hospitalized, and the failure to notify was due to a breakdown in the surveillance system.
232.Rationale: While the Discussion mentions clinical awareness as a potential factor in misdiagnosis, the Passage Body explicitly states that the failure to notify was due to a 'breakdown in the surveillance system,' not misdiagnosis. This distractor introduces an external causal factor (clinical awareness) not supported by the evidence in the Passage Body, making it a plausible but invalid inference.

232.Option: The high proportion of rabies cases in children under 15 years of age suggests that the current surveillance system is more effective at detecting cases in younger populations.
232.Discussion_Source: 12 of the 14 cases of human rabies occurred in children under the age of fifteen years, as did 37.8% of the animal bites.
Evidence: None of the 14 cases of human rabies detected were notified through the routine surveillance system.
The majority of cases occurred in children under 15, but all were unreported.
232.Rationale: This distractor reverses the implication of the data. The high proportion of child cases is presented as a risk factor, not a signal of surveillance effectiveness. The correct inference is about under-reporting, not differential detection. This causal reversal misinterprets the relationship between age distribution and surveillance reliability, making it a strong but invalid distractor.

232.Option: The poor quality of records at Prophylaxis Centers is the primary reason for the under-reporting of rabies cases in Mozambique.
232.Discussion_Source: A major limitation of our study was the poor quality of records of cases of animal bites at the Prophylaxis Centers.
Evidence: None of the 14 cases of human rabies detected were notified through the routine surveillance system.
The failure to notify was due to a breakdown in the surveillance system.
232.Rationale: This distractor misattributes the cause of under-reporting to record quality, which is a limitation of the study but not the reason for the failure to report rabies cases. The Passage Body explicitly states the failure was due to a 'breakdown in the surveillance system,' not data quality. This external dependency on a methodological caveat makes it a plausible but unsupported inference.

232.Option: The absence of rabies cases in the routine surveillance system during the outbreak indicates that the surveillance system is functioning effectively by excluding false positives.
232.Discussion_Source: None of the 14 cases of human rabies detected were notified through the routine surveillance system.
Evidence: None of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
The investigation revealed a total of 14 cases during the outbreak.
232.Rationale: This distractor inverts the meaning of surveillance failure. The correct inference is that the system failed to detect real cases, not that it successfully filtered out false ones. The statement implies a positive function of the system, which contradicts the evidence. It is a speculative misinterpretation of surveillance performance, making it a strong distractor based on a reversal of logic.

233.Option: The chronic inflammatory process observed in human CBM patients may be due to an unbalanced immune response that fails to transition from Th17 to Th1 dominance, resulting in persistent fungal burden and inadequate regulation by Treg cells.
233.Discussion_Source: A state of chronic or intractable fungal disease may be the result of an unbalanced immune response which compromises the host's ability to cope with fungal infective cells rather than an 'intrinsic' susceptibility to infection [36].
Evidence: In the footpad, the IL-17+ population was higher than IFN-γ+ cells in the first 7 days of infection (E). The opposite occurs after 21 days, when the IFN-γ+ population is higher than IL-17+ T cells (F), reversing the ratio between these populations.
Foxp3+ Treg cells also expand during the infection, being more numerous in dLN (Fig 1G).

Neutralization of IL-17 highly increased CFUs at day 7 post-infection (Fig 3D). In turn, neutralization of IFN-γ increased CFUs at days 7 and 14 (Fig 3G). Although not found to be statistically significant, neutralization of IFN-γ showed a trend towards increased CFUs vs. control mice at later time points, allowing fungal persistence up to 28 days after infection.

Depletion of CD25 + cells reduced CFUs in the footpad at day 14 post-infection but delayed disease resolution... mice treated with anti-CD25 mAb were not able to reduce their fungal load during the late stage of the infection, favoring the persistence of the fungus in the tissue (Fig 2E).
233.Rationale: This distractor extrapolates the murine model findings to human disease pathogenesis, introducing a causal claim about human chronicity that is not supported by the passage. While the Discussion mentions that chronic disease may result from immune imbalance, the passage body only presents data from a self-healing murine model. The inference about human disease mechanisms requires external clinical data not provided in the passage, making this an external dependency distractor.

233.Option: The initial Th17 response in the footpad is primarily responsible for the recruitment of neutrophils, which are essential for the early elimination of fungal conidia and hyphae, while Th1 cells become critical later for clearing muriform cells.
233.Discussion_Source: IL-17 secreted by Th17 cells promotes neutrophils’ maturation and migration to the site of infection, conferring protection against extracellular pathogens [41,42], whereas IFN-γ produced by the Th1 population properly activates neutrophils and enhances fungal cells’ phagocytosis [43].
Evidence: In the footpad, the IL-17+ population was higher than IFN-γ+ cells in the first 7 days of infection (E). The opposite occurs after 21 days, when the IFN-γ+ population is higher than IL-17+ T cells (F), reversing the ratio between these populations.
Neutralization of IL-17 highly increased CFUs at day 7 post-infection (Fig 3D). In turn, neutralization of IFN-γ increased CFUs at days 7 and 14 (Fig 3G).
Dectin-2-/- mice have reduced Th17 cell responses and higher fungal burden at the chronic phase of experimental CBM
233.Rationale: This distractor misattributes the mechanism of fungal clearance to neutrophil recruitment as the primary driver of early clearance, which is not directly supported by the passage. While the Discussion links Th17 to neutrophil recruitment, the passage body does not establish that neutrophils are the main effector cells responsible for clearance. The inference relies on speculative mechanistic interpretation not grounded in the experimental data, making it a speculation-based distractor.

233.Option: The expansion of Treg cells in the draining lymph node during infection suggests a suppressive environment that limits early immune activation, thereby delaying initial fungal control but promoting long-term resolution.
233.Discussion_Source: High doses of IL-17 in lesions essentially represent a host strategy against fungal infections. However, an average Th17 response may overlap the regulatory role of Tregs, and this imbalance may eventually result in a less effective response to the fungus [16].
Evidence: Foxp3+ Treg cells also expand during the infection, being more numerous in dLN (Fig 1G).

Depletion of CD25 + cells reduced CFUs in the footpad at day 14 post-infection but delayed disease resolution... mice treated with anti-CD25 mAb were not able to reduce their fungal load during the late stage of the infection, favoring the persistence of the fungus in the tissue (Fig 2E).
233.Rationale: This distractor reverses the causal relationship implied in the correct option. The correct inference is that Treg expansion supports resolution by modulating inflammation and enabling effective Th1 responses. This distractor frames Treg expansion as suppressive and delaying early control, which contradicts the experimental evidence: Treg depletion reduced early fungal burden but impaired late clearance. The passage shows Tregs are necessary for resolution, not for delaying it, making this a causal reversal distractor.

233.Option: The failure to sustain a Th17 response beyond 14 days in the murine model indicates that this subset is not essential for long-term fungal control, as its decline coincides with disease remission.
233.Discussion_Source: In the footpad, the IL-17+ population was higher than IFN-γ+ cells in the first 7 days of infection (E). The opposite occurs after 21 days, when the IFN-γ+ population is higher than IL-17+ T cells (F), reversing the ratio between these populations.
Evidence: Neutralization of IL-17 highly increased CFUs at day 7 post-infection (Fig 3D).
Neutralization of IFN-γ increased CFUs at days 7 and 14 (Fig 3G).

Depletion of CD25 + cells reduced CFUs in the footpad at day 14 post-infection but delayed disease resolution... mice treated with anti-CD25 mAb were not able to reduce their fungal load during the late stage of the infection, favoring the persistence of the fungus in the tissue (Fig 2E).
233.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the temporal decline of Th17 as evidence of irrelevance, ignoring the functional data showing that neutralizing IL-17 at day 7 increases fungal load. The correct inference is that Th17 is critical early, and its decline is part of a coordinated shift, not a failure. This distractor ignores the experimental evidence of Th17's functional role and instead treats its disappearance as a sign of non-essentiality, constituting a causal reversal of the actual immune dynamics.

234.Option: The presence of multiple E. bieneusi genotypes in both symptomatic and asymptomatic children indicates that clinical symptoms are not reliably associated with specific genotypes.
234.Discussion_Source: In the Zambézia province, E. bieneusi was found in 3 asymptomatic children. Two of them lived in a rural area, attended the same school, but were infected by different genotypes (Peru11 and S2) of the parasite. Both children were co-infected with G. duodenalis, Blastocystis sp., and Strongyloides spp. The third child was infected by the Type IV genotype of E. bieneusi and was co-infected with G. duodenalis. None of the symptomatic children investigated in this province tested positive to E. bieneusi. In Maputo province, E. bieneusi was found in six male children, one presenting with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 5 asymptomatic.
Evidence: None of the symptomatic children investigated in this province tested positive to E. bieneusi.
In Maputo province, E. bieneusi was found in six male children, one presenting with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 5 asymptomatic.
The overall prevalence for those with symptoms was 0.3% (1/331, 95% CI: 0.04–2.1), and those with diarrhoea 0.4% (1/251, 95% CI: 0.06–2.8).
234.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the limited data on symptom association. While one symptomatic child was positive, the sample size is too small to draw conclusions about genotype-specific symptomatology. The passage does not provide enough data to infer that symptom status is unrelated to genotype, nor does it compare symptom profiles across genotypes. This conclusion overreaches the evidence and is not supported by the data, making it a plausible but invalid inference.

234.Option: The high genetic diversity of E. bieneusi in Mozambique, as evidenced by the identification of a novel genotype, suggests that the parasite is undergoing rapid evolution in this region.
234.Discussion_Source: Nucleotide sequence analysis on the ITS region of the nine positive cases revealed the presence of four genotypes, three previously reported (Peru11, Type IV, and S2) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1). This constitutes the first report of these genotypes in Mozambique.
Evidence: A novel genotype (named HhMzEb1) was identified.
The novel genotype HhMzEb1 differed from genotype D (AF101200) by one nucleotide.
No mixed infections were observed.
234.Rationale: The discovery of a novel genotype does not imply rapid evolution. The passage provides no information about mutation rates, evolutionary timelines, or population dynamics. The single nucleotide difference is not sufficient to infer evolutionary speed. This distractor confuses genetic novelty with evolutionary process, which is a common misinterpretation in molecular epidemiology and thus serves as a plausible but unsupported inference.

234.Option: The detection of E. bieneusi genotypes in both rural and urban settings suggests that transmission is primarily driven by environmental contamination rather than animal contact.
234.Discussion_Source: In Zambézia, 807 asymptomatic schoolchildren and 290 children presenting with gastrointestinal complaints seeking for medical care were investigated in 10 and six districts of the province, respectively (Table 2 and S1 and S2 Tables). The overall male/female ratio was 1.0, and children in the age group 6 to 10 years-old represented 60% of the surveyed individuals. Three out of four children investigated lived in rural areas. In the rural Manhiça district, 109 asymptomatic children and 41 children presenting with gastrointestinal complaints (11 with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 30 with less severe diarrhoea) and seeking for medical care were investigated (Table 2 and S3 and S4 Tables). In this subpopulation, the male/female ratio was 1.1, and children in the age group 12–23 months-old represented 40.7% of the surveyed individuals. Most children (62.7%) were from the area covered by the MDH. All these children lived in rural areas.
Evidence: All children in both provinces lived in rural areas.
The study was conducted in rural districts of Zambézia and Manhiça.
No data on urban transmission or environmental sampling were provided.
234.Rationale: The passage does not include any data on urban transmission or environmental contamination. The study population was entirely rural, so no comparison between rural and urban settings exists. The conclusion about environmental transmission versus animal contact cannot be derived from the data. This distractor introduces an unsupported comparison and misattributes causality, making it a strong but invalid inference based on the passage.

234.Option: The fact that all identified genotypes belong to Group 1 implies that E. bieneusi in Mozambique is primarily transmitted through direct human-to-human contact.
234.Discussion_Source: All genotypes identified in the present study, including novel HhMzEb1, belonged to Group 1 (Fig 1) that includes E. bieneusi genotypes with low host specificity that are found not only in humans but also in domestic and wild animals worldwide [10,11,14].
Evidence: Group 1 genotypes have low host specificity and are found in both humans and animals.
Genotype Type IV has been reported in a wide range of animal hosts including non-human primates, cattle, cats, dogs, rodents, birds, snakes, and black bears.
Peru11 has been found in a baboon in Kenya and in various animals globally.
234.Rationale: This distractor reverses the correct inference. The passage explicitly states that Group 1 genotypes have low host specificity and are found in both humans and animals, which supports zoonotic transmission. The conclusion that transmission is primarily human-to-human contradicts the evidence of animal reservoirs. This causal reversal misrepresents the epidemiological significance of host specificity and is a strong distractor because it uses correct terminology but inverts the logical relationship.

235.Option: The high rates of antifilarial antibodies in primary school children across most PHIs suggest that transmission assessment surveys are highly sensitive for detecting ongoing filariasis transmission in endemic areas.
235.Discussion_Source: Antifilarial antibodies were detected in primary school children in 17 of 19 PHIs.
Evidence: Antifilarial antibodies were detected in primary school children in 17 of 19 PHIs
CFA rates in school aged children were much lower than those in adults
All EUs easily passed TAS criteria for stopping MDA
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs
235.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the relationship between antibody presence and transmission detection. The Discussion explicitly states that antibody rates are higher than antigenemia rates and that antibody testing may not be feasible for routine surveillance. The presence of antibodies in children does not indicate active transmission, especially since the study shows that antigenemia is much lower in children. The correct inference is that TAS is insensitive, not that it is highly sensitive. This distractor reverses the intended inference by equating antibody detection with reliable transmission detection, which contradicts the evidence that TAS failed to detect transmission despite positive findings in more sensitive methods.

235.Option: The fact that none of the children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia indicates that transmission assessment surveys are reliable indicators of the absence of active filarial transmission.
235.Discussion_Source: None of the 16 children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia.
Evidence: None of the 16 children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs
CFA rates were higher in adults than in children
Community CFA rates were <2% in 17 of 19 PHIs, but upper confidence limits for CFA were >2% in 5 of 19 PHIs
235.Rationale: This distractor incorrectly assumes that the absence of microfilaremia in antigen-positive children confirms the absence of transmission. However, the Passage Body and Discussion clarify that amicrofilaremic antigenemia is common in post-MDA settings and does not rule out persistent infection. The presence of filarial DNA in mosquitoes in multiple PHIs directly contradicts the idea that transmission has been eliminated. This distractor fails to recognize that antigenemia without microfilaremia can still reflect low-level ongoing transmission, making it a flawed inference based on a single data point without considering broader evidence.

235.Option: The successful completion of five rounds of MDA in Sri Lanka, combined with high MDA compliance rates, ensures that transmission assessment surveys are sufficient to confirm the interruption of lymphatic filariasis transmission.
235.Discussion_Source: All EUs met criteria for conducting TAS by having completed 5 rounds of MDA in 2006 with high MDA compliance rates (>80%).
Evidence: All EUs met criteria for conducting TAS by having completed 5 rounds of MDA in 2006 with high MDA compliance rates (>80%)
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs
CFA rates were higher in adults than in children
Community CFA rates were <2% in 17 of 19 PHIs, but upper confidence limits for CFA were >2% in 5 of 19 PHIs
235.Rationale: This distractor assumes that high MDA compliance and completion of rounds are sufficient to validate TAS outcomes. However, the Discussion explicitly argues that even with successful MDA, TAS may not detect ongoing transmission. The evidence shows that despite high compliance and multiple rounds, filarial DNA was detected in mosquitoes in 10 PHIs, and CFA rates remained elevated in adults. This distractor ignores the key finding that TAS is not sensitive enough to detect low-level persistence, making it a misleading conclusion based on external program success rather than actual surveillance performance.

235.Option: The detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes in some PHIs suggests that transmission assessment surveys are overly conservative and should be abandoned in favor of molecular xenodiagnosis for all post-MDA evaluations.
235.Discussion_Source: Results from this study strongly support the use of molecular xenodiagnosis for post-MDA surveillance in areas where LF is transmitted by Culex mosquitoes.
Evidence: Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs
All EUs easily passed TAS criteria for stopping MDA
Molecular xenodiagnosis requires skilled personnel, specialized facilities, and funding
The sensitivity of TAS might be improved by reducing the size of EUs or by sampling adults instead of school-aged children
235.Rationale: This distractor overgeneralizes the role of molecular xenodiagnosis by suggesting it should replace TAS entirely. The Discussion acknowledges that MX is resource-intensive and not feasible for all programs, and recommends it as a complementary tool, not a replacement. The correct inference is that TAS is insufficient and should be supplemented, not that it should be abandoned. This distractor misrepresents the Discussion’s balanced recommendation by advocating for a complete shift in strategy based on partial evidence, making it a plausible but invalid inference.

236.Option: The integration of mHealth tools with community health volunteers enables the collection of high-quality photographic data for therapeutic response assessment, which is as accurate as in-person evaluation and supports the scalability of digital health interventions in rural settings.
236.Discussion_Source: However, we have recently shown that evaluation by photographs is highly accurate [12].
Evidence: In the intervention (app) arm, the study physician evaluated the therapeutic response via photographs taken by the CHV using the phone app at four time points.
In the control (standard of care) groups, evaluation of therapeutic response was conducted in-person by a physician at the CIDEIM clinic.

In the intervention (app) arm, it was not possible to evaluate the induration of lesions via photographs, only their epithelization, but induration is not considered an absolute criterion of cure [20].
236.Rationale: While the passage states that photographic evaluation is sensitive and specific relative to in-person assessment, the conclusion overgeneralizes by claiming 'high-quality' data and 'scalability'—concepts not directly supported by the evidence. The question focuses on patient follow-up, not diagnostic accuracy. This distractor misrepresents the study's primary endpoint and shifts focus to a secondary methodological finding, making it plausible but not derivable from the passage alone.

236.Option: Despite the challenges of poor connectivity and mobility, the mHealth intervention led to a complete resolution of treatment non-adherence in the intervention arm, enabling full therapeutic monitoring for all patients.
236.Discussion_Source: Even in the intervention arm, 46.9% had been lost to follow-up by week 26, reflecting difficulty in mobility in the study area for geographical and socioeconomic reasons.
Evidence: Of the 75 participants in the two randomized arms, 74 had information on whether or not they were followed up at target study week 26.
26/49 (53.1%) were evaluated by active follow-up at this time in the intervention arm.
The median number of adverse events per person was 1 in each arm and under each treatment.
236.Rationale: This distractor falsely claims 'complete resolution of treatment non-adherence' and 'full therapeutic monitoring for all patients,' which directly contradicts the data showing 46.9% loss to follow-up. The correct inference acknowledges improvement despite limitations. This distractor reverses the evidence by implying full success, making it a strong but invalid inference based on the passage.

236.Option: The addition of a non-randomized control group enhanced the internal validity of the trial by allowing for more robust comparisons between the intervention and standard care, particularly in assessing long-term treatment outcomes.
236.Discussion_Source: Another potential limitation of the study is that a non-randomized control group was added, and comparisons with this group are restricted in terms of internal validity.
Evidence: The study was expanded in order to enroll such patients and hence increase study recruitment.
The first patient in this non-randomized control group was enrolled in January 2017.
Comparisons with this group are restricted in terms of internal validity.
236.Rationale: This distractor misrepresents the Discussion by claiming the non-randomized group improved internal validity, when the passage explicitly states the opposite. The question focuses on the impact of the mHealth intervention on follow-up, not study design limitations. This distractor uses a plausible-sounding claim about research methodology but contradicts the passage, making it a strong external dependency distractor.

236.Option: The mHealth intervention significantly reduced the burden on health facilities by shifting the responsibility for treatment monitoring to community health volunteers, thereby improving access to care in remote areas.
236.Discussion_Source: The combination of mHealth and empowerment of CHV through training to effectively utilize the app, responded to an unmet patient need and provided previously unavailable information to the health system on the effectiveness of the treatments administered as standard of care.
Evidence: The intervention arm had a higher proportion of patients monitored at week 26 compared to standard care.
CHV were able to move more freely, providing outreach to dispersed communities.
The study was closed in December 2017, when the target total sample size of the randomized arms had been reached.
236.Rationale: While the passage notes that the intervention shifted monitoring to CHVs and improved data collection, it does not provide evidence that this reduced the burden on health facilities. The conclusion about 'reducing burden' is speculative and not supported by data on facility workload, staffing, or resource use. This distractor introduces a new causal claim about system-level impact not derivable from the passage, making it a plausible but invalid inference.

237.Option: The spatial clustering of high household density and lower SES in coastal Kenya is primarily driven by proximity to main roads and aquatic habitats favorable for Anopheles mosquitoes and snails.
237.Discussion_Source: In contrast, people with lower SES were usually clustered away from the main roads (Figure 5, Figure S1 in Text S1). Low-income houses were clustered closer to sites that were suitable for snail hosts of S. haematobium (Figure 5 and Figure S1 in Text S1).
Evidence: In Vuga and Kinango, the clustering pattern of both density and low income overlapped.
Low-income houses were clustered closer to sites that were suitable for snail hosts of S. haematobium (Figure 5 and Figure S1 in Text S1).
Clustering of high household density were often located near the main road (Figure 5, Figure S1 in Text S1).
237.Rationale: This distractor misrepresents the spatial relationship between SES, household density, and environmental risk factors. The passage states that low-income households were clustered away from main roads but near snail habitats, while high-density clusters were near main roads. The distractor incorrectly combines these patterns into a single causal narrative, implying that both density and SES are driven by proximity to roads and aquatic sites, which is not supported by the data. The conclusion requires a synthesis of spatial patterns not directly stated in the passage and misattributes causality.

237.Option: The lack of significant correlation between mosquito abundance and malaria or filariasis cases suggests that vector control measures are ineffective in reducing transmission in coastal Kenya.
237.Discussion_Source: However, we did not find a significant correlation between mosquito abundance (or presence) and malaria or filarial infections. These results were likely affected by the limitations of the methods used to collect mosquitoes—based on our long-term surveillance for the present study, we previously detailed [77] that when abundance of mosquitoes is low, it is difficult to obtain a representative sample of local mosquito populations using classical sampling methods.
Evidence: We did not find a significant correlation between mosquito abundance (or presence) and malaria or filarial infections.
These results were likely affected by the limitations of the methods used to collect mosquitoes...
When abundance of mosquitoes is low, it is difficult to obtain a representative sample...
237.Rationale: This distractor draws a causal conclusion about the ineffectiveness of vector control from a null finding that is explicitly attributed to methodological limitations. The passage acknowledges that the lack of correlation may be due to poor sampling sensitivity, not a true absence of transmission dynamics. The conclusion overreaches by inferring policy implications (ineffectiveness of control) from a technical limitation, which cannot be derived from the passage alone.

237.Option: The high prevalence of co-infections in children and young adults is primarily due to the prior mass drug administration (MDA) campaign in 2003, which reduced hookworm and Trichuris infections in the 10–19 age group.
237.Discussion_Source: A mass drug administration (MDA) had been performed in the study area in 2003 as part of the National Programme for Elimination of Lymphatic Filariasis (NPELF) [70], consisting of treatment with diethylcarbamazine citrate (DEC) and albendazole, which also has a de-worming effect for STH. This prior administration of albendazole may also have reduced the prevalence of hookworms in Milalani and Nganja residents, where prevalence was lowest in the 10–19 age group. Following drug administration, re-infection with Trichuris and Ascaris is rapid (less than one year to reach pre-treatment prevalence), but is longer for hookworm [79], which may explain why we did not find a similar likely effect of NPELF on Trichuris infection.
Evidence: This prior administration of albendazole may also have reduced the prevalence of hookworms in Milalani and Nganja residents, where prevalence was lowest in the 10–19 age group.
Re-infection with Trichuris and Ascaris is rapid (less than one year to reach pre-treatment prevalence), but is longer for hookworm.
The high prevalence of co-infections in children and young adults was observed across all villages, not just Milalani and Nganja.
237.Rationale: This distractor misattributes the overall trend in co-infection prevalence to a single past MDA campaign, despite the passage noting that the effect was limited to specific villages and that re-infection dynamics vary. The passage explicitly states that the high co-infection burden in children and young adults is a general pattern across villages, not solely due to the 2003 MDA. The conclusion reverses the causal direction by implying the MDA caused the age-specific pattern, while the passage only suggests it may have contributed to a localized reduction in hookworm prevalence.
