The inference about improved trust is speculative and not supported by data on community attitudes, participation rates, or post-screening follow-up. This distractor misattributes a social outcome to the screening strategy without empirical support in the passage.

427.Option: The high detection rate of non-NTD skin conditions indicates that the screening strategy was primarily effective in identifying and managing common dermatoses rather than neglected tropical diseases.
427.Discussion_Source: The majority of cases were other non-NTD skin conditions, including fungal infections (Pityriasis versicolor, Tinea capitis, Tinea pedis), eczema and traumatic lesions. These conditions can be reliably diagnosed clinically by trained health workers.
Evidence: The majority of cases were other non-NTD skin conditions...
These conditions can be reliably diagnosed clinically by trained health workers.
427.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the significance of non-NTD findings. The Passage Body emphasizes that the integrated approach allowed treatment of all skin conditions, including non-NTD dermatoses, which was ethically important and reduced under-reporting. The high proportion of non-NTD cases does not imply inefficacy for NTDs; rather, it reflects the broad clinical scope. The inference that the strategy was 'primarily effective' for non-NTD conditions contradicts the study’s goal of integrated NTD surveillance and overlooks the value of early detection of rare but severe NTDs.

427.Option: The absence of confirmed yaws cases in the study area suggests that yaws has been eradicated in southern Benin.
427.Discussion_Source: In this study, no case of yaws was confirmed. However, it is too early to state that there are no yaws in this rural district and screening should continue.
Evidence: In this study, no case of yaws was confirmed.
However, it is too early to state that there are no yaws in this rural district and screening should continue.
427.Rationale: This distractor reverses the correct inference by claiming eradication based on a single screening round. The Discussion explicitly states that the absence of confirmed cases does not rule out the presence of yaws, and continued screening is recommended. The inference that yaws has been eradicated is unsupported and contradicts the cautionary language in the Discussion. This is a clear causal reversal of the evidence.

427.Option: The low number of confirmed NTD cases indicates that the integrated screening strategy was inefficient and not cost-effective in this rural setting.
427.Discussion_Source: In our study, we screened 1,106 people to detect only 15 BU cases (1.4%) and 3 cases of leprosy (0.3%). ... Although the number of BU and leprosy cases which were confirmed and treated may seem low, these cases were detected early. Indeed, only 20% of the BU cases detected had Category III lesions and one out of the 3 leprosy cases had a Grade 2 disability. Early detection helps reduce long costly hospital stay, the functional limitations and the socio-economic consequences [28–32].
Evidence: Although the number of BU and leprosy cases which were confirmed and treated may seem low, these cases were detected early.
Early detection helps reduce long costly hospital stay, the functional limitations and the socio-economic consequences.
427.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the efficiency of the screening strategy by focusing solely on low detection numbers without considering clinical outcomes. The Passage Body explicitly argues that early detection—despite low case counts—reduces long-term burden, which implies cost-effectiveness. The inference that the strategy was inefficient contradicts the study’s own justification and the discussion of benefits. This is a causal reversal that ignores the value of early intervention in preventing severe disease.

428.Option: The triangular test is chosen because it allows for simultaneous randomisation between multiple experimental treatments and standard care, enabling a platform approach to testing multiple therapies in sequence.
428.Discussion_Source: Implementations of GOST that allow simultaneous randomisation between multiple experimental treatments and S are also possible.
Evidence: The triangular test is just one of many sequential methods that could be used as the engine to drive GOST.

A “platform approach” was suggested for trials of a series of experimental treatments in Ebola virus disease [11]. First a comparison of Treatment E1 with S is conducted. If Treatment E1 wins it becomes the new standard. Treatment E2 is then compared with the current standard, and so on.
GOST could be used as the design for each comparison made within the platform approach, with α being set at 0.025 throughout.
428.Rationale: This distractor misattributes a feature of the platform trial design to the triangular test itself. The passage states that simultaneous randomisation is possible in GOST implementations, but this is not a property of the triangular test per se. The triangular test is described as the statistical engine, not the mechanism enabling platform designs. The correct inference focuses on the test's efficiency in sample size minimization under moderate effect sizes, not its compatibility with multi-arm designs.

428.Option: The triangular test ensures that the trial will stop quickly if the experimental treatment is harmful, thereby protecting patient safety and preventing further exposure to ineffective therapies.
428.Discussion_Source: The design does not seek to distinguish between lack of effect and harm: either way there is no further interest in E and resources are better devoted to other experimental treatments.
Evidence: The design does not seek to distinguish between lack of effect and harm: either way there is no further interest in E and resources are better devoted to other experimental treatments.

Adoption of the GOST design should be subject to approval of a Data and Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB), who consider unblinded data during the ongoing trial. They have the duty to recommend stopping the trial if they feel it unsafe to continue...
428.Rationale: This distractor reverses the causal logic of the passage. The triangular test does not actively detect harm; instead, it stops when there is no evidence of benefit, regardless of whether the treatment is harmful or ineffective. The actual safety monitoring is delegated to the DSMB, not the statistical test. The correct option emphasizes efficiency under moderate benefit, while this distractor incorrectly implies the test is designed for harm detection, which contradicts the passage's explicit statement.

428.Option: The triangular test is preferred because it maintains high power (0.90) even when the true treatment effect is small (odds ratio of 1.5), ensuring that clinically meaningful benefits are not missed.
428.Discussion_Source: For an increase in success rate from ½ on S to ⅗ on E (R = 1.5), GOST will conclude that E is better than S with probability 0.47.
Evidence: For an increase in success rate from ½ on S to ⅗ on E (R = 1.5), GOST will conclude that E is better than S with probability 0.47.
The design is constructed to ensure that, if R = 2, then the probability that E wins is 0.90.
The triangular test is chosen because amongst tests satisfying the power requirement above, it minimises the maximum expected sample size, which occurs when R is close to 1.5 [12].
428.Rationale: This distractor misrepresents the power of the trial at R = 1.5. The passage explicitly states that power is only 0.47 at R = 1.5, which is far below the 0.90 target. The correct inference is that the triangular test is efficient when the effect is moderate (R ≈ 1.5), not that it maintains high power at that level. This distractor confuses power with efficiency and misinterprets the statistical properties, making it a plausible but invalid inference based on the passage.

428.Option: The triangular test is designed to reduce the risk of false positives by using a one-sided α level of 0.025, which is more conservative than standard two-sided testing.
428.Discussion_Source: The null hypothesis is that E has no effect, in which case PEj = PSj and so Rj = 1 for each value of j from 1 to k– 1. If this null hypothesis is true then the probability of concluding that E is better than S (an event that will be designated “E wins” hereafter) is set to equal 0.025. This is the one-sided risk of type I error (denoted by α), and the value of 0.025 is chosen for GOST to follow convention.
Evidence: The null hypothesis is that E has no effect, in which case PEj = PSj and so Rj = 1 for each value of j from 1 to k– 1.
If this null hypothesis is true then the probability of concluding that E is better than S (an event that will be designated “E wins” hereafter) is set to equal 0.025.
The triangular test is chosen because amongst tests satisfying the power requirement above, it minimises the maximum expected sample size, which occurs when R is close to 1.5 [12].
428.Rationale: This distractor focuses on the choice of α = 0.025 as a conservative measure, but the passage does not claim this is more conservative than two-sided testing. The one-sided α = 0.025 is a convention in this design, not a general rule for reducing false positives. The key advantage of the triangular test lies in minimizing expected sample size under moderate effect, not in error rate control. This distractor misrepresents the rationale for the α level and shifts focus from the core efficiency argument to a peripheral statistical detail.

429.Option: The area where infection was acquired was associated with L-AmB effectiveness in univariate analysis, suggesting that geographic origin may be a key determinant of treatment success.
429.Discussion_Source: In univariate analysis (Table 2), the area where infection was acquired was associated with L-AmB effectiveness (p≤ .05).
Evidence: In univariate analysis (Table 2), the area where infection was acquired was associated with L-AmB effectiveness (p≤ .05).
Being infected with L. infantum approached but did not quite achieve significance (p = .057).
In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).
429.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the strength of evidence by elevating the univariate association with geographic origin as the most consistent predictor. However, the multivariable analysis—considered the gold standard for identifying independent predictors—shows that geographic area was not significant after adjusting for species. The correct inference requires recognizing that only L. infantum remained significant in the adjusted model, making this a classic case of overreliance on univariate results.

429.Discussion_Source: The following variables L. infantum species, area (New World or Old World) where infection was acquired, mucocutaneous form, and frontline therapy with L-AmB were included in multivariable conditional logistic regression analysis. In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).
Evidence: The following variables L. infantum species, area (New World or Old World) where infection was acquired, mucocutaneous form, and frontline therapy with L-AmB were included in multivariable conditional logistic regression analysis.
In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).

The passage explicitly states that only L. infantum remained significant in multivariable analysis, making this a direct misrepresentation of the statistical findings. It exploits the inclusion of the variable in the model to suggest importance without evidence.

429.Discussion_Source: Of the 9 patients with L. infantum tegumentary leishmaniasis, 5 (55%) had mucocutaneous and 4 (45%) localized cutaneous leishmaniasis.
Evidence: Of the 9 patients with L. infantum tegumentary leishmaniasis, 5 (55%) had mucocutaneous and 4 (45%) localized cutaneous leishmaniasis.
In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).
We observed no significant difference in efficacy in patients with or without mucosal involvement which provides some post-hoc justification to this approach.
429.Rationale: This distractor reverses the causal inference by assuming that mucocutaneous disease leads to better outcomes, when the passage explicitly states there was no significant difference in efficacy between mucocutaneous and localized forms. The correct inference is that species—not disease form—drives outcome. This distractor misinterprets the clinical data by conflating disease type with treatment success, despite the passage stating no such association was found.

429.Option: The high rate of adverse events, including renal toxicity and infusion-related reactions, suggests that liposomal amphotericin B is too risky for use in travelers with tegumentary leishmaniasis.
429.Discussion_Source: Renal toxicity and infusion related reactions occurred in 15 (35%) and 6 (14%) patients respectively. While 53% of patients (23/43) experienced at least one adverse event, seven of them (30%) required modification of L-AmB therapy during follow-up.
Evidence: Renal toxicity and infusion related reactions occurred in 15 (35%) and 6 (14%) patients respectively.
While 53% of patients (23/43) experienced at least one adverse event, seven of them (30%) required modification of L-AmB therapy during follow-up.
In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).
429.Rationale: This distractor introduces a value judgment about risk-benefit that is not supported by the passage. While adverse events are reported, the discussion emphasizes that the risk-to-benefit ratio may be lower in TL than in VL, but does not conclude that L-AmB is too risky. The correct inference is about predictive factors for success, not safety. This distractor shifts focus to safety concerns, which are not the subject of the question, and misrepresents the study’s conclusion as a blanket recommendation against use.

430.Option: The third intervention—annual mass vaccination of replacements for a decade followed by a decade of a test and slaughter strategy—is the most cost-effective approach for controlling brucellosis in India.
430.Discussion_Source: Although the third approach–i.e. annual mass vaccination of replacements for a decade followed by a decade of a test and slaughter strategy–was also found to be cost-effective, it is less likely to be adopted in India because it is a Hindu majority country and Hindus consider cows to be sacred. As a result, cow slaughter is banned in most states of India.
Evidence: The BCR for the first, second and the third intervention for cattle were 7.98 (6.29; 10.09), 10.62 (8.33; 12.5) and, 3.16 (2.66; 3.83), respectively.
For buffaloes, similar results were obtained (Table 3).

The results indicate that first 20 years of the programme will be cost-effective for all scenarios with the second intervention (vaccination of replacements with mass vaccination at the beginning of the program) being a significantly more cost-effective choice.
430.Rationale: This distractor misrepresents the economic efficiency of the third intervention by ignoring the quantitative evidence from the benefit-cost ratio (BCR) and net present value (NPV). The correct option is supported by the highest BCR (10.62) and high NPV, while the third intervention has the lowest BCR (3.16) and is explicitly described as less likely to be adopted due to cultural and religious barriers. The statement is plausible because it references a real intervention from the discussion, but it contradicts the economic data presented in the passage, making it a strong distractor based on external dependency (cultural context) rather than economic metrics.

430.Option: The first intervention—annual mass vaccination of replacement calves only—is the most cost-effective approach for controlling brucellosis in India.
430.Discussion_Source: The results indicate that first 20 years of the programme will be cost-effective for all scenarios with the second intervention (vaccination of replacements with mass vaccination at the beginning of the program) being a significantly more cost-effective choice.
Evidence: The BCR for the first, second and the third intervention for cattle were 7.98 (6.29; 10.09), 10.62 (8.33; 12.5) and, 3.16 (2.66; 3.83), respectively.
For buffaloes, similar results were obtained (Table 3).
430.Rationale: This distractor incorrectly identifies the first intervention as the most cost-effective, despite the passage clearly stating that the second intervention has a significantly higher BCR (10.62 vs. 7.98). The statement is plausible because it references a real intervention and the passage confirms that all scenarios are cost-effective, but it fails to recognize that BCR is the primary measure of efficiency. This distractor misinterprets the relative ranking of interventions based on economic outcomes, making it a valid causal reversal of the correct inference.

430.Option: The second intervention is the most cost-effective because it leads to the highest net present value (NPV) over the 20-year period.
430.Discussion_Source: For cattle, the NPV was found to be US $ 4·16 billion (95% CI: US $ 3·16; 5·39 billion) for the scenario 1, US $ 8·31 (6·40; 9·87) billion for the scenario 2 and, US $ 4·26 (3·26; 5·61) for the third scenario (Table 2).
Evidence: The BCR for the first, second and the third intervention for cattle were 7.98 (6.29; 10.09), 10.62 (8.33; 12.5) and, 3.16 (2.66; 3.83), respectively.

The results indicate that first 20 years of the programme will be cost-effective for all scenarios with the second intervention (vaccination of replacements with mass vaccination at the beginning of the program) being a significantly more cost-effective choice.
430.Rationale: This distractor incorrectly attributes the superiority of the second intervention to NPV alone, while the passage emphasizes BCR as the key metric for cost-effectiveness. Although the second intervention has a high NPV ($8.31 billion), the BCR (10.62) is the more appropriate measure for efficiency because it accounts for cost per unit of benefit. The statement is plausible because it uses real data from the passage, but it misrepresents the reasoning behind the conclusion by focusing on NPV instead of BCR, which is the correct economic indicator for efficiency. This is a valid causal reversal based on misinterpreting the relative importance of economic metrics.

430.Option: The second intervention is the most cost-effective because it results in the fastest reduction in disease prevalence across both cattle and buffalo populations.
430.Discussion_Source: The results suggest that all of the three approaches investigated for controlling the disease would be beneficial as the prevalence of Brucella infection will drop below 2% in cattle and 3% in buffalo after 20 years of the implementation of disease control program.
Evidence: For each intervention, our results indicate that the prevalence of Brucella infection will drop below 2% in cattle after 20 years of the implementation of disease control program (Fig 1) for the cattle population. For buffaloes, a similar trend was observed.
The second intervention (vaccination of replacements with mass vaccination at the beginning of the program) being a significantly more cost-effective choice.
430.Rationale: This distractor confuses disease prevalence reduction with cost-effectiveness. The passage states that all three interventions lead to similar long-term prevalence reductions (below 2% in cattle, below 3% in buffalo) after 20 years, so no intervention is shown to be faster in reducing prevalence. The conclusion about cost-effectiveness is based on BCR and NPV, not on the speed of prevalence decline. The statement is plausible because it references a real outcome (prevalence reduction) and the second intervention is indeed effective, but it misattributes the reason for its superiority to a factor not supported by the data. This is a valid causal reversal that misinterprets the evidence.

431.Option: The high incidence of leptospirosis in the Metropolitan of Porto Alegre and Centro Oriental Rio Grandense mesoregions reflects the effectiveness of the current surveillance system in identifying and reporting cases in high-risk areas.
431.Discussion_Source: Hotspot analysis proved to be useful for identifying clusters of leptospirosis, as this analysis identified clusters located in the mesoregions with the highest incidence rates in the state: Metropolitan of Porto Alegre (4.08 cases/100,000 inhabitants) and Centro Oriental Rio Grandense (16.7 cases/100,000 inhabitants).
Evidence: In 220 municipalities, cases of the disease were not recorded in the years evaluated.
This finding may reflect the failure in the sensitivity of the state’s leptospirosis surveillance system.
The distribution of leptospirosis incidence rates in Rio Grande do Sul varied greatly in the different regions of the state.
431.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the surveillance data as evidence of system effectiveness, when the Discussion explicitly suggests that the absence of cases in 220 municipalities may indicate underreporting and poor surveillance sensitivity. The correct inference requires recognizing that high incidence in some areas does not imply reliable reporting, especially given the large number of unreported municipalities. This distractor reverses the causal relationship between cluster detection and system performance.

431.Option: The seasonal pattern of leptospirosis incidence from January to April is likely due to increased outdoor activity and higher human exposure to contaminated environments during the warmer months.
431.Discussion_Source: In the present study, leptospirosis seasonality was observed from January to April, with a higher incidence in the hottest months.
Evidence: The SARIMA model predicted a decrease in incidence in 2020.
The model considered the 12-month seasonal pattern and predicted a decrease in incidence in 2020 (Fig 5).
The discussion does not provide causal explanations for seasonality beyond climate and rainfall patterns.
431.Rationale: While the seasonal timing is noted, the Discussion does not attribute seasonality to human behavior or outdoor activity. Instead, it references environmental factors like rainfall and temperature, and cites studies linking incidence to wetlands and precipitation. This distractor introduces a speculative behavioral explanation not supported by the passage, making it an example of external dependency and speculation.

431.Option: The lethality rate in Rio Grande do Sul has remained stable over time, indicating consistent healthcare access and treatment quality across the state.
431.Discussion_Source: On the other hand, this mesoregion had the highest mortality rates due to leptospirosis from 2007 to 2019, with an average of 0.61 deaths/100,000 inhabitants, which was higher than the average rates for the entire country... The lethality rate was lower at 4.0% in the Centro Oriental Rio Grandense mesoregion. On the other hand, this mesoregion had the highest mortality rates...
Evidence: The average lethality rate was 5.3% in Rio Grande do Sul, while it was 8.6% in the Metropolitan of Porto Alegre mesoregion.
The discussion does not provide time-series data on lethality rates over the 13-year period.
The discussion highlights regional disparities in lethality, not temporal stability.
431.Rationale: The correct inference focuses on incidence trends and predictive modeling, not lethality. This distractor fabricates a conclusion about temporal stability in lethality, which is not supported by the data. The Discussion only reports average lethality and regional differences, not changes over time, making this a clear case of speculation and misinterpretation of data.

431.Option: The SARIMA model's prediction of a decrease in incidence in 2020 is likely due to the implementation of new public health interventions in 2019, which were not accounted for in the model.
431.Discussion_Source: The model considered the 12-month seasonal pattern and predicted a decrease in incidence in 2020 (Fig 5).
Evidence: The discussion does not mention any public health interventions in 2019.
The model was validated using data from 2019, and the predicted values were compared to actual values.
The discussion states that the model's adequacy was confirmed by Theil-U and MAPE, but does not attribute the forecast to external interventions.
431.Rationale: This distractor introduces a causal explanation (new interventions) that is not present in the passage. The Discussion acknowledges model adequacy and prediction accuracy but does not reference any policy changes or interventions. This is a speculative inference based on external knowledge, making it a strong distractor that appears plausible but cannot be derived from the passage alone.

432.Option: The clinical overlap between TSM-associated IRIS and other opportunistic infections makes it difficult to distinguish IRIS from treatment failure or adverse drug reactions, necessitating comprehensive diagnostic evaluation.
432.Discussion_Source: In the case of suspected IRIS, clinicians must make a comprehensive judgment to exclude the possibility of new infections, treatment failure, and adverse drug reactions.
Evidence: TSM-associated IRIS was diagnosed as follows: (1) the exacerbation of clinical symptoms specifically attributed to TSM after ART initiation, and (2) exclusion of the possibility of treatment failure, drug-related adverse effects, and new infections.
The diagnosis of TSM-associated IRIS requires both (1) and (2) to be met, and all diagnoses are made independently by two experienced physicians.
432.Rationale: While the passage emphasizes the diagnostic challenge of distinguishing IRIS from other conditions, the conclusion about the need for comprehensive evaluation is a clinical recommendation not directly inferable from the comparative outcomes. The question focuses on the impact on care and healthcare resources, not diagnostic difficulty. This distractor misaligns with the question's focus by shifting attention to diagnostic uncertainty rather than resource utilization.

432.Option: The earlier onset of TSM-associated IRIS within 2 weeks of ART initiation suggests that immune recovery occurs more rapidly in patients with TSM compared to those with other fungal infections.
432.Discussion_Source: We found that TSM-associated IRIS occurred predominantly within 2 weeks after ART initiation, indicating an earlier onset than other IRIS, underscoring the need for vigilant against TSM-associated IRIS soon after ART initiation.
Evidence: In 24 IRIS patients, the median occurrence time was 9.0 days (IQR, 5.0–16.8 days) after ART initiation. Among them, 70.9% (21/24) occurred within 2 weeks of ART initiation.
The study did not compare the timing of IRIS onset across different fungal infections in a direct statistical or clinical manner.
432.Rationale: This distractor makes a causal inference about the rate of immune recovery based on timing, which is not supported by the data. The passage only states that TSM-associated IRIS occurs earlier than other IRIS types but does not provide evidence about the underlying immune dynamics or comparative recovery speed. The conclusion misinterprets temporal onset as a proxy for immune recovery rate, which is a speculative leap beyond the passage.

432.Option: The lack of significant differences in baseline CD4+ counts between IRIS and non-IRIS groups suggests that immunosuppression severity is not a risk factor for TSM-associated IRIS.
432.Discussion_Source: Previous studies profound immunosuppression in HIV/AIDS patients before ART initiation associated with a higher risk of IRIS [6,7,19–22]. In our study, the majority of patients were severe immunodeficiency. However, no significance was observed in baseline CD4+ count between IRIS and Non-IRIS groups.
Evidence: No significant differences were observed between two groups regarding demographic characteristics, co-infection status, the interval between antifungal and ART initiation, CD4+ counts, and blood test results at baseline (Tables 2 and S2).
The discussion notes that previous studies link immunosuppression to IRIS risk, but this study found no association at baseline.
432.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the absence of a statistically significant difference as definitive evidence that immunosuppression is not a risk factor. The passage acknowledges the study's limitations, including potential underpowering and lack of subgroup analysis, which undermines the ability to rule out such a relationship. The conclusion overreaches by asserting a negative finding based on non-significant data, which is a common flaw in statistical reasoning.

432.Option: The similar mortality rates between IRIS and non-IRIS groups indicate that TSM-associated IRIS does not significantly affect patient outcomes or healthcare burden.
432.Discussion_Source: No difference in mortality rates between two groups (0 vs. 6.0%, P > 0.05) (Table 4).
Evidence: No difference in mortality rates between two groups (0 vs. 6.0%, P > 0.05) (Table 4).
Compared with non-IRIS group, IRIS group had a longer hospital stay (41.0 days vs. 33.0 days, P = 0.001).
In IRIS group, 12 patients (50.0%) experienced readmission events, compared with 41 patients (20.5%) in non-IRIS group, indicating a significantly higher readmission rate in IRIS group (P = 0.001).
432.Rationale: This distractor reverses the correct inference by dismissing the clinical and resource burden of IRIS based solely on mortality. The correct conclusion recognizes that even without increased death, prolonged hospitalization and higher readmission rates constitute a significant burden. This distractor misrepresents the meaning of 'impact' by equating it only with mortality, ignoring other key indicators of healthcare strain. It is a causal reversal in reasoning: it downplays the burden despite clear evidence of increased resource use.

433.Option: The migration route of AC in nonpermissive host mice is identical to that in permissive host rats, with the same timing of organ arrival and consistent pathological outcomes across both host types.
433.Discussion_Source: Here, we first provide strong experimental evidence that AC L3 used the same migration route in mice as in rats, but the timeline was slightly different. AC migrated from the liver to the lung at 2 hpi in rats but at 4 hpi in mice, which might be caused by the faster blood flow rate in the rat liver and lung than in the mouse liver and lung [33].
Evidence: AC migrated from the liver to the lung at 4 hpi in mice, but at 2 hpi in rats.
In mice, AC L3 migration caused fatal pneumonia during the acute and early infection phases, while in rats, no such fatal pneumonia was reported during these phases.
The study found that AC induced persistent but nonfatal interstitial pneumonia in rats during the late infection phase, whereas in mice, fatal pneumonia occurred during the early phase.
433.Rationale: This distractor incorrectly assumes identical timing and pathological outcomes between host types. The Passage Body explicitly shows differences in migration timing (4 hpi vs. 2 hpi) and, more critically, divergent pathogenic effects: fatal pneumonia in mice but not in rats during early infection. The correct inference focuses on the unique pathogenicity in nonpermissive hosts, not a shared pattern across hosts.

433.Option: The primary cause of death in nonpermissive host mice infected with high-dose AC is eosinophilic meningitis, which develops during the late infection phase and leads to irreversible neurological damage.
433.Discussion_Source: As it is well known that humans and mice infected by AC develop eosinophilic meningitis, an increasing number of studies in recent decades have focused on characterizing and searching for therapeutic strategies for AC-induced meningitis [30]. We should pay the most attention to severe eosinophilic meningitis, which can cause death and permanent irreversible nerve damage [31], but this manifestation was observed only in the late phase of AC infection (21 dpi for mice).
Evidence: Mice infected with 300 AC L3 died at 1 dpi, 2 dpi, and 3 dpi, well before 21 dpi.
H&E staining of the mouse brain at 1 dpi showed normal tissue structure, while the lung showed severe alveolar congestion and inflammatory cell infiltration.
The study states that fatal pneumonia occurred during the acute and early infection phases, before the onset of eosinophilic meningitis.
433.Rationale: This distractor misattributes the cause of death to eosinophilic meningitis, which occurs only in the late infection phase (21 dpi). However, the experimental data show that high-dose infection leads to death within 3 days, with no brain pathology observed at 1 dpi. The correct inference is that lung pathology, not meningitis, is responsible for early mortality.

433.Option: The immune response in nonpermissive host mice during the early infection phase is dominated by a Th1-type response, which is essential for controlling AC infection and preventing fatal pneumonia.
433.Discussion_Source: In this study, we clarified that in mice, Th1 and Th2 responses were both enhanced in the acute phase of AC infection (1 dpi), characterized by upregulation of TNF-α, IL-1β, and INOS in Th1 cells and IL-6 and IL-13 in Th2 cells. However, after the acute phase, the elevated Th1 cytokines dropped to normal levels, but the elevated Th2 cytokines were persistently upregulated.
Evidence: Th1 cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, INOS) were elevated at 1 dpi but decreased to normal by 2–4 dpi.
Th2 cytokines (IL-6, IL-13) were significantly upregulated at 1 dpi and remained elevated.
Activated Stat3 and p65 were upregulated at 1 dpi, and Stat3/IL-6 signaling was shown to mediate pneumonia.
The study found that inhibition of Stat3/IL-6 signaling attenuated pneumonia, indicating a pathogenic role for Th2 bias.
433.Rationale: This distractor reverses the correct immunological inference. While Th1 activation occurs early, the data show that the dominant and pathogenic response is Th2-biased, with IL-6 and Stat3 activation driving fatal pneumonia. The correct inference is that the Th2 response, not Th1, is central to early pathogenesis, making this a causal reversal distractor.

433.Option: Pulmonary fibrosis is a common and severe outcome of AC infection in nonpermissive host mice during the late infection phase, resulting from sustained inflammation and collagen deposition.
433.Discussion_Source: In contrast, we observed fibrosis only in the rat lung after 28 dpi but not in the mouse lung. The main reason, on the one hand, was that mice infected by AC died before 28 dpi and had insufficient time for fibrosis development.
Evidence: No fibrosis was observed in mouse lungs at any time point, including 21 dpi.
Mice infected with 30 AC L3 died before 28 dpi due to fatal pneumonia.
The study explicitly states that fibrosis was not observed in mouse lungs, only in rat lungs during the late phase.
433.Rationale: This distractor misapplies findings from permissive hosts (rats) to nonpermissive hosts (mice). The Passage Body clearly states that fibrosis was not observed in mice, despite sustained inflammation. The correct inference is that fibrosis is absent in nonpermissive hosts due to early mortality, not a general outcome of late infection.

434.Option: The antibody response in mice infected with O. tsutsugamushi Gilliam strain is dominated by IgG2c, suggesting a Th1-driven immune response that may be insufficient to prevent persistent inflammation in target organs.
434.Discussion_Source: The antibody response was dominated by the IgG2c isotype, suggesting the importance of Th1 responses in this model. However, IgG1 was detected in all groups except after low dose i.v. infection, which is indicative of Th2 immune response involvement as well.
Evidence: The antibody response was dominated by the IgG2c isotype, suggesting the importance of Th1 responses in this model.
However, IgG1 was detected in all groups except after low dose i.v. infection, which is indicative of Th2 immune response involvement as well.

The peak liver inflammatory index occurred at 12–15 dpi for mid and low dose i.v. route (Fig 5B and 5C). Following i.d. inoculation, the peak liver inflammatory index occurred at 12–18 dpi, and although the lesions decreased in diameter and quantity, they were still present at the experimental endpoint of 30 dpi (Fig 5D).

The kidney inflammatory ratio continued to intensify through the experimental endpoint, 15 dpi after i.v. high and mid-dose inoculation (Fig 6B). After i.v. low dose inoculation, the kidney inflammatory ratio plateaued at 6 dpi and remained elevated through 15 dpi. The peak of the kidney inflammatory ratio after i.d. inoculation occurred at 18 dpi and was unresolved at day 30 pi.
434.Rationale: This distractor introduces a speculative causal link between IgG2c dominance and insufficient control of inflammation, which is not supported by the passage. The Discussion notes that IgG2c suggests Th1 involvement, but also acknowledges Th2 involvement via IgG1. The passage does not claim that Th1 responses are inadequate; rather, it suggests that both arms may contribute to immune homeostasis. The inference about 'insufficient' control contradicts the correct interpretation that inflammation persistence reflects effective immune activity. The question focuses on the temporal relationship between bacterial load and inflammation, not antibody isotype dynamics.

434.Option: The sustained lung inflammation observed after the peak of bacterial load is likely due to ongoing bacterial replication in the pulmonary tissue, indicating incomplete pathogen clearance.
434.Discussion_Source: Statistically significant lung inflammation was observed concurrently irrespective of dose or route and was sustained after the peak of infection, suggesting elicitation by a host-mediated inflammatory response.
Evidence: Statistically significant lung inflammation was observed concurrently irrespective of dose or route and was sustained after the peak of infection, suggesting elicitation by a host-mediated inflammatory response.
The peak of lung bacterial load for the i.v. high dose recipient mice was observed at 9 dpi (Fig 4B), which was the day of onset of weight loss (Fig 1A).
After i.d. inoculation, the peak lung pathology score observed at 18–30 dpi (Fig 6C).
The peak of bacterial load after i.d. inoculation of mice reached the same magnitude as that of the i.v. high and mid-dose; however, it occurred nine days later in the infection at 18 dpi.
434.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the temporal relationship by assuming that sustained inflammation implies ongoing bacterial replication. However, the passage explicitly states that lung inflammation persists after the peak of infection and is 'sustained after the peak of infection,' which contradicts the idea of active replication. The Discussion directly attributes the sustained inflammation to a 'host-mediated inflammatory response,' not ongoing infection. This reversal of cause-effect (inflammation as a result of infection vs. inflammation as a sign of immune control) makes it a strong causal reversal distractor.

434.Option: The delayed onset of peak inflammation in the liver and kidney after i.d. inoculation compared to i.v. inoculation suggests that the intradermal route leads to a less effective immune response and greater tissue damage.
434.Discussion_Source: Following i.d. inoculation, the peak liver inflammatory index occurred at 12–18 dpi, and although the lesions decreased in diameter and quantity, they were still present at the experimental endpoint of 30 dpi (Fig 5D). The peak of the kidney inflammatory ratio after i.d. inoculation occurred at 18 dpi and was unresolved at day 30 pi.
Evidence: The peak liver inflammatory index occurred at 12–15 dpi for mid and low dose i.v. route (Fig 5B and 5C). Following i.d. inoculation, the peak liver inflammatory index occurred at 12–18 dpi, and although the lesions decreased in diameter and quantity, they were still present at the experimental endpoint of 30 dpi (Fig 5D).

The kidney inflammatory ratio continued to intensify through the experimental endpoint, 15 dpi after i.v. high and mid-dose inoculation (Fig 6B). After i.v. low dose inoculation, the kidney inflammatory ratio plateaued at 6 dpi and remained elevated through 15 dpi. The peak of the kidney inflammatory ratio after i.d. inoculation occurred at 18 dpi and was unresolved at day 30 pi.

The model recapitulates the pathology that has been observed in humans with scrub typhus, which may be correlated with documented clinical manifestations including labored breathing, pulmonary edema, cardiac dysfunction, hepatomegaly, elevations of serum hepatic aminotransferases, and acute renal failure [22,23,24,28,65,66].
434.Rationale: This distractor incorrectly interprets delayed inflammation as evidence of a weaker or less effective immune response. However, the passage does not compare the quality or effectiveness of immune responses between routes. The Discussion emphasizes that both routes produce analogous pathology and that the model mimics human disease. The persistence of inflammation after i.d. inoculation is not framed as a sign of failure but as a feature of the immune response. The inference about 'less effective' response is unsupported and represents a misreading of the temporal pattern as a measure of immune competence.

434.Option: The lack of eschar formation in the i.d. inoculation model suggests that the immune response in this route is delayed or ineffective in containing the initial infection at the inoculation site.
434.Discussion_Source: In contrast to models utilizing non-human primates, no eschar was formed after i.d. inoculation in our murine model [71,72,73].
Evidence: In contrast to models utilizing non-human primates, no eschar was formed after i.d. inoculation in our murine model [71,72,73].

The i.d. route of infection results in a hematogenously disseminated systemic infection as occurs in human scrub typhus, but bypasses the events of early cutaneous infection and the initial spreading steps.

The distribution of Orientia is directly influenced by the route of inoculation. It has been shown that intramuscular and subcutaneous inoculation results in a disseminated infection involving Kupffer cells and macrophages [57,58].
434.Rationale: This distractor assumes that the absence of an eschar indicates immune failure at the inoculation site. However, the Discussion explicitly states that the lack of eschar is expected in mice and does not imply ineffective immunity. The model is designed to mimic systemic dissemination, and the absence of eschar is noted as a known difference from non-human primates, not a sign of poor immune control. The inference about 'delayed or ineffective' response is not supported by the passage and reflects an external dependency on primate models without evidence from the study data.

435.Option: The high prevalence of T. trichiura infections in rural West Malaysia is primarily due to the widespread use of ineffective anthelminthic drugs and the development of drug resistance in the parasite population.
435.Discussion_Source: The higher rate of T. trichiura infection has been reported to be due to the ineffective dosage and choice of anthelminthic used. Currently, the recommended treatment regime for STH infection is broad spectrum anthelminthics such as albendazole and mebendazole. Important therapeutic differences do exist between these drugs which affect their uses in clinical practice [22]. Both drugs are effective against ascariasis in single dose, whereas single doses of either albendazole or mebendazole have been found to be ineffective in many cases of trichuriasis [22]. Furthermore, potential resistance of T. trichiura to anthelminthic drugs has been highlighted in two intervention studies in Malaysian communities [23], [24].
Evidence: The higher rate of T. trichiura infection has been reported to be due to the ineffective dosage and choice of anthelminthic used.
Potential resistance of T. trichiura to anthelminthic drugs has been highlighted in two intervention studies in Malaysian communities [23], [24].
435.Rationale: This distractor overstates the causal role of drug resistance based on limited evidence. While the Discussion mentions potential resistance and ineffective treatment, it does not confirm that resistance is the primary cause of high T. trichiura prevalence. The Passage Body does not provide data on drug efficacy or resistance patterns, and the conclusion misrepresents a speculative hypothesis as a definitive explanation. It also ignores other key risk factors like sanitation and re-infection, which are central to the correct inference.

435.Option: The significantly lower prevalence of IPIs in Sungai Layau village is due to the absence of domestic animals and the exclusive use of treated water, which together eliminate the primary transmission routes for intestinal parasites.
435.Discussion_Source: In Sungai Layau village where each family was provided with a concrete house and basic amenities like treated water supply, prevalence of IPIs was shown to be significantly lower (4.5%).
Evidence: In Sungai Layau village where each family was provided with a concrete house and basic amenities like treated water supply, prevalence of IPIs was shown to be significantly lower (4.5%).
Rearing of animals such as pigs, chickens, ducks, dogs, cats and monkeys are common practices.
435.Rationale: This distractor incorrectly attributes the low prevalence solely to the absence of animals and treated water, which is not supported by the text. The Passage Body notes that animals are common in other villages but does not state they are absent in Sungai Layau. The Discussion only links improved infrastructure (including treated water) to lower prevalence, not the absence of animals. This distractor misrepresents a correlation as a direct causal mechanism and ignores other contributing factors like sanitation and behavior.

435.Option: Restoring mass deworming programs alone is sufficient to control intestinal parasitic infections in rural West Malaysia, given the high re-infection rates observed after treatment.
435.Discussion_Source: As the present findings have indicated that the overall prevalence was 73.2%, it is strongly recommended that mass deworming programmes are restored and a systematic evaluation of treatment regime must be put in place to reduce the rates of re-infection.
Evidence: Local studies among rural communities have found that re-infection can occur as early as 2 months post treatment, by 4 months almost half of the treated population had been re-infected [24] and by 6 months the intensity of infections had returned to pre-treatment levels [26].
it is strongly recommended that mass deworming programmes are restored and a systematic evaluation of treatment regime must be put in place to reduce the rates of re-infection.
435.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the recommendation by implying that mass deworming alone is sufficient. The Discussion explicitly states that a systematic evaluation of treatment is needed and that re-infection is a major challenge, suggesting that deworming must be combined with other measures. The correct inference requires integrating deworming with broader environmental and behavioral interventions, which this distractor omits, making it a causal reversal of the intended public health strategy.

435.Option: The most effective way to reduce IPIs in rural West Malaysia is to focus on treating protozoan infections with targeted antiparasitic drugs, given their high prevalence and association with severe diarrhea in children.
435.Discussion_Source: As for protozoa infections, the overall prevalence was 21.4%. However, in contrast with the latest local study in rural area, Noor Azian and colleagues reported very high rates of protozoa infection (72.3%) [29]. The present study found that G. duodenalis (10.4%) was the most predominant protozoa, followed by E. histolytica/dispar (10.2%) and lastly Cryptosporidium sp. (2.1%).
Evidence: The overall prevalence of IPIs among 716 participants was 73.2% with STH infections (73.2%; 524) being significantly more common compared to protozoa infections (21.4%; 153) (p<0.001).
The present study found that G. duodenalis (10.4%) was the most predominant protozoa, followed by E. histolytica/dispar (10.2%) and lastly Cryptosporidium sp. (2.1%).
435.Rationale: This distractor misrepresents the relative burden of infections. The Passage Body clearly shows that STH infections (73.2%) are far more prevalent than protozoa (21.4%), and the Discussion does not elevate protozoa as the primary target. The conclusion focuses on a less common infection type and ignores the dominant role of STH and the broader environmental and socioeconomic factors. It also fails to address the key evidence about re-infection and infrastructure, making it a misleading prioritization based on incomplete data.

436.Option: The rapid and drastic decrease in catch of tsetse suggests that tiny targets can control G. f. fuscipes in this setting, and this success may be due to the small size of the area, well-defined habitat, and low reinvasion pressure.
436.Discussion_Source: The rapid and drastic decrease in catch of tsetse suggests that tiny targets can control G. f. fuscipes in this setting. The small size of the area, well defined with limited habitat for tsetse and its relative isolation with low reinvasion pressure may have contributed to the success of the intervention.
Evidence: The total catch of tsetse from 44 sentinel traps was 145 tsetse (1.62 flies/trap/day). At the first evaluation post target deployment (T1, 2 months later), only 2 tsetse were caught (0.02 tsetse/trap/day) were observed (Fig 3).
The model surmises that transmission fell from around 311 new infections per year in 2013 to a mean of 7 and 0 new infections per year in 2014 and 2015 respectively.
436.Rationale: This statement is a plausible explanation for the entomological success of the intervention but cannot be derived as an inference about the *relative contribution* of intervention strategies to the decline in HAT transmission. The question focuses on the comparative impact of medical vs. vector control, not the ecological or geographical factors enabling vector control. While the passage mentions these factors, they are speculative and not tied to the quantitative attribution of transmission decline.

436.Option: Despite the high number of people screened and treated, the addition of vector control was necessary to achieve a substantial decline in HAT reporting, suggesting that medical interventions alone are insufficient to interrupt transmission in persistent foci.
436.Discussion_Source: Despite this effort, a substantial decrease in HAT reporting occurred only when vector control was added to the medical strategy.
Evidence: The total number of cases diagnosed and treated (1143 cases since 2009 out of a population of 38 674 people) suggests that up to 3.0% of the people in this area have had sleeping sickness.

A huge medical effort was conducted in this focus with 142 467 people being screened between 2009 and 2015. Given the population number is ~38 674, it means that on average, every individual in the area was screened nearly four times during this period.
436.Rationale: While the statement is consistent with the passage, it misrepresents the inference required by the question. The correct inference is about the *relative contribution* of vector control versus medical interventions to the decline in transmission, not a general claim about the insufficiency of medical interventions. This distractor shifts focus to a broader conclusion about medical strategy limitations, which is not directly supported by the quantitative model data on contribution percentages.

436.Option: The improvement in passive surveillance in 2015 likely led to a higher number of detected cases, which may have masked the true impact of vector control on reducing transmission.
436.Discussion_Source: The improved passive surveillance that began in 2015 could skew this ratio.
Evidence: In 2014, there was a decrease in the stage 1 to stage 2 detection ratio for both active and passive surveillance.
Modelling results indicate that 29.6% (95% CI: 5–49%) of this two-year decline was through active screening, and the remaining 70.4% (95% CI: 51–95%) was due to vector control.
436.Rationale: This distractor introduces speculation about the confounding effect of enhanced surveillance on case detection, which is mentioned in the Discussion but not supported by the evidence in the Passage Body. The question asks for an inference based on the *decline in transmission*, not on the interpretation of case detection patterns. The model explicitly accounts for changes in detection rates, and the evidence shows a clear decline in new infections independent of detection changes, making this a misleading causal reversal.

436.Option: The model predicts that continued screening and vector control will lead to elimination as a public health problem by 2018, which is earlier than the WHO 2020 target, indicating that the current strategy is highly effective.
436.Discussion_Source: Continuing this combined strategy is predicted to result in fewer than 2 annually reported cases and zero transmission in 2020; this strategy appears sufficient to reach elimination as a public health problem by 2018 and full elimination over a decade in advance of the WHO 2030 goal.
Evidence: The model surmises that transmission fell from around 311 new infections per year in 2013 to a mean of 7 and 0 new infections per year in 2014 and 2015 respectively.
Modelling results indicate that 29.6% (95% CI: 5–49%) of this two-year decline was through active screening, and the remaining 70.4% (95% CI: 51–95%) was due to vector control.
436.Rationale: This statement is a plausible future projection but does not address the *relative contribution* of interventions to the *decline observed in 2014–2015*. The question focuses on the past two-year decline, not future projections. While the model predicts early elimination, this inference is not derived from the evidence about the *proportional impact* of each intervention, making it a valid distractor based on external dependency and misaligned temporal focus.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence of CCHFV IgG antibodies in older animals is primarily due to longer-lasting antibody persistence in cattle and camels compared to sheep and goats.
437.Discussion_Source: There are no experimental data for camelids to date. Therefore, more long-term CCHFV infection studies of livestock animals are necessary to improve our understanding of the humoral and cellular immunological memory in host species.
Evidence: There are no experimental data for camelids to date.
There are no experimental data on the CCHFV antibody persistence in livestock and wildlife, determining the extent of this effect.
The steady rise of antibody prevalences with increasing age in all species can be due to an additive effect.
437.Rationale: This distractor assumes that antibody persistence differs between species, which is not supported by the passage. The Discussion explicitly states that no data exist on antibody persistence in any livestock species, including camels. Therefore, attributing the age-related trend to longer persistence in certain species is speculative and cannot be derived from the evidence. The correct inference relies on exposure, not immune memory duration.

437.Option: The observed age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence is mainly driven by differences in slaughter age, as young animals are more likely to be slaughtered before reaching high seroprevalence levels.
437.Discussion_Source: Sheep and goats represent an important source of meat in Mauritania and are therefore slaughtered at an early age, while cattle and camels are often used as dual-purpose breeds.
Evidence: Sheep and goats represent an important source of meat in Mauritania and are therefore slaughtered at an early age, while cattle and camels are often used as dual-purpose breeds.

Due to the fact that female camels produce the largest amount of milk between the 6th and 8th parity (i.e. 10–12 years of age) [37], they reach a high average age and are often slaughtered late at the age of 18–20 years when milk yields start to decrease gradually.
437.Rationale: While the Discussion notes differences in slaughter age, it explicitly states that age effects alone cannot explain prevalence differences between species. The correct inference emphasizes cumulative exposure, not slaughter timing. This distractor misattributes the pattern to selective removal of young animals, which contradicts the evidence that even young animals show higher seroprevalence in cattle and camels compared to small ruminants.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence in older animals is due to increased tick infestation rates in older livestock, which are more susceptible to vector-borne transmission.
437.Discussion_Source: Higher age coincides with a greater chance of exposure to CCHFV-positive ticks in endemic regions and thus becoming infected with the virus.
Evidence: Higher age coincides with a greater chance of exposure to CCHFV-positive ticks in endemic regions and thus becoming infected with the virus.

It was shown for cattle [13] and small ruminants [38] that nomadic grazing will significantly increase seroprevalences due to higher tick exposition risks compared to stationary trough feeding systems.
437.Rationale: This distractor misinterprets the causal direction by suggesting that older animals have higher tick infestation rates, which is not stated or implied in the passage. The evidence only supports that older animals have had more time to be exposed, not that they are more infested. The correct inference is about cumulative exposure over time, not increased susceptibility or infestation in older animals.

437.Option: The age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence is likely due to the use of a non-specific ELISA assay that produces false positives in older animals.
437.Discussion_Source: Two serological reactors from camels from Australia may either be non-specific immune responses or represent cross-reactions to other Orthonairoviruses circulating in Australia.
Evidence: Two serological reactors from camels from Australia may either be non-specific immune responses or represent cross-reactions to other Orthonairoviruses circulating in Australia.
The detection of a distinct immune response of the immunized alpaca from the 14th day post immunization onwards supports the validity of the ELISA in terms of specific reactions (Table 2).
With two exceptions, all negative reference sera were clearly negative.
437.Rationale: This distractor introduces a methodological flaw (non-specific assay) not supported by the evidence. The Discussion confirms the ELISA's validity through positive control responses in immunized alpacas and the near-universal negativity of Australian controls. The two anomalous results were noted as possible cross-reactions but do not undermine the overall assay reliability. Thus, this conclusion cannot be derived from the passage and misrepresents the validation data.

438.Option: The integration of trachoma screening into annual door-to-door mass treatment campaigns for onchocerciasis, schistosomiasis, and geo-helminthiasis enabled the identification of suspected trichiasis cases across all regions of Togo, including previously unsurveyed districts.
438.Discussion_Source: As part of the mass treatment activities conducted door-to-door in all regions of the country, Community Health Workers were trained to identify suspected cases of trichiasis. The door-to-door strategy provided a national overview of the situation, particularly in the Plateaux (Akébou, Wawa, Amou, Danyi, Kpélé, Kloto, Ogou, Moyen Mono and Haho) and Maritime (Yoto, Avé, Zio, Lacs, Vo and Bas- Mono) districts, which had not benefited from a prevalence survey for active or sequelae trachoma.
Evidence: Community Health Workers were trained to identify suspected cases of trichiasis during door-to-door mass treatment campaigns.
The door-to-door strategy provided a national overview of the situation, particularly in the Plateaux and Maritime districts, which had not benefited from a prevalence survey.
438.Rationale: While the door-to-door campaigns were instrumental in expanding surveillance reach, the question asks for the key development that enabled Togo to meet the international criteria for elimination. The 2017 survey using the WHO-recommended Tropical Data protocol was the definitive, methodologically rigorous assessment that provided the evidence required for validation. The door-to-door screening contributed to case detection but was not the basis for meeting the formal elimination thresholds. This distractor misattributes the primary validation mechanism to a supportive activity rather than the confirmatory survey.

438.Option: The political commitment demonstrated by the establishment of the National Program for Blindness Control in 1989 laid the foundation for the long-term success of trachoma elimination in Togo.
438.Discussion_Source: Political commitment was reflected early in the fight with the establishment of the National Program for Blindness control in 1989.
Evidence: The National Program for Blindness control was created in 1989 by the Ministry of Health.
The SAFE strategy was adopted and implemented through this program.
438.Rationale: Although early political commitment was important, the Passage Body and Discussion make clear that the actual elimination milestone was achieved through specific, recent, and methodologically sound activities—particularly the 2017 survey and the subsequent validation process. The 1989 program was a precursor, not the key development that met the international criteria. This distractor confuses historical groundwork with the specific, evidence-based event that enabled validation, making it a plausible but unsupported inference.

438.Option: The use of the Tropical Data protocol in the 2017 survey allowed for the accurate identification of trichiasis cases, which led to the successful surgical management of all identified patients.
438.Discussion_Source: The 2017 survey conducted in the 7 districts according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by the WHO revealed a prevalence of TF of less than 1% in all districts among children aged 1 to 9 years and a very low prevalence of TT among 15-year-olds (Table 3). At the end of the survey, 27 patients were identified in need for surgery. 19 of them were surgered while 6 refused surgery and therefore benefited from epilation of eyelashes. In Tchamba district, one (01) patient diagnosed had died before the surgery period and one (01) other person was absent in Anié district during the surgery period.
Evidence: 27 patients were identified in need for surgery.
19 were operated on, 6 refused surgery, one died before surgery, and one was absent.
438.Rationale: This distractor misrepresents the outcome of the 2017 survey by claiming all identified patients were successfully managed. However, the passage explicitly states that not all patients were operated on—some refused, one died, and one was absent. The key development was not the surgical management itself, but the accurate prevalence data from the survey that met WHO thresholds. This distractor focuses on a secondary outcome (surgery) while ignoring the primary requirement: demonstrating sub-threshold prevalence through a validated survey.

438.Option: The correction of methodological weaknesses in earlier surveys, such as the overdiagnosis of trachomatous follicular inflammation in 2006 and 2009, enabled the 2017 survey to produce reliable data for elimination validation.
438.Discussion_Source: We noted that a false start was made in the evaluation of the epidemiological situation of trachoma in Togo. Indeed, the 'integrated threshold mapping' of 2006 in the Binah district in the Kara region and of 2009 in 14 health districts out of the 15 in the three northern regions of Togo (Centrale, Kara and Savanes) with the exception of the Binah district was alarming in revealing TF prevalences that exceeded the elimination threshold for active trachoma. These results were probably due to overdiagnosis, with the presence of a high number of follicles of any size and in any conjunctival location being erroneously considered as indicative of TF, as explicitly acknowledged in the 2009 publication [9]. The 2011 results, five (05) years after the 2006 results and two years after the 2009 results despite the absence of mass treatment, confirm the overdiagnosis hypothesis. The more rigorous 2011 surveys found near absence of active trachoma in all three districts especially in the districts where TF prevalences were highest.
Evidence: The 2006 and 2009 surveys likely suffered from overdiagnosis of TF.
The 2011 survey used a more rigorous methodology and found near absence of active trachoma.
Methodological weaknesses related to the indicators to be collected were found in the surveys run between 2006 and 2016.
438.Rationale: While correcting earlier methodological flaws was important for improving data quality, the key development that enabled elimination validation was the 2017 survey conducted using the WHO-recommended Tropical Data protocol. The 2011 survey was a step toward better methodology but did not meet the full validation criteria. This distractor confuses a necessary improvement in data quality with the actual milestone event—validation based on a standardized, WHO-endorsed survey. It is plausible but not directly supported as the primary enabling factor for elimination certification.

439.Option: Deficiencies in IL-7 signaling have been linked to other chronic diseases such as HIV, and IL-7 therapy has been suggested to improve T cell survival in these patients, suggesting that IL-7 supplementation could be beneficial in visceral leishmaniasis.
439.Discussion_Source: Deficiencies in IL-7 signaling have been linked to other chronic diseases such as HIV, and IL-7 therapy has been suggested to improve T cell survival in these patients [29].
Evidence: Our data does not support impaired IL-7 signaling as an explanation for loss of CD4+ T cells during VL.

While we show that additional rhIL-7 stimulation increased (already heightened) pSTAT5 in VL patient CD4+ T cells compared to EC, it is uncertain if manipulation of the IL-7 signaling pathway would improve cell survival in VL patients.

When comparing pSTAT5 in CD38+ compared to CD38- CD4+ T cells, less pSTAT5 was seen in the CD38+ CD4+ T cell subset from VL patients, where the pSTAT5 levels were similar in the two subsets in ECs, suggesting that there is a feedback mechanism to downregulate IL-7 signaling upon activation.
439.Rationale: This distractor assumes that because IL-7 therapy helps in HIV, it would also help in VL. However, the passage explicitly states that IL-7 signaling is not impaired in VL and that experimental attempts to improve survival with rhIL-7 were ineffective. The conclusion relies on external evidence (HIV) not present in the passage and misapplies it to VL without considering the unique immunological context described.

439.Option: The reduction in soluble IL-7Rα in VL patients is a direct result of increased membrane-bound IL-7Rα expression on CD4+ T cells, leading to enhanced IL-7 signaling and T cell survival.
439.Discussion_Source: The reduction in sIL-7Rα was accompanied by increased levels of IL-7 in plasma from VL patients (D0) compared to EC and VL post treatment (D30). Increased plasma level of IL-7 is a sign of lymphopenia [30,31], something frequently observed in VL patients [32]
Evidence: The lack of correlation between transcriptional data and RT-qPCR data, was unexpected but may be explained by the use of different individuals in the different assays combined with that the differences in RNA seq and Nanostring results seen between groups were not the most pronounced (logFC -1.39 and -0.719 respectively).

While we found no significant reduction IL7RA mRNA in VL by qPCR, previous studies reporting on transcriptional profiling of cells from VL patients found, in line with the observation made in TB patients, reduced IL7RA mRNA levels in CD4+ T cells from VL patients compared to ECs [17,18].
The increased IL-7 signaling may be a response to the lymphopenia to support the survival of existing T cells in VL.
439.Rationale: This distractor assumes a direct causal link between increased surface IL-7Rα and decreased soluble IL-7Rα, which is not supported by the passage. The discussion notes that the biological role of sIL-7Rα is unclear and that its reduction may reflect increased membrane binding, but this is speculative. The passage does not confirm this mechanism, and the data show complex, non-linear dynamics across mRNA, protein, and signaling levels.

439.Option: The elevated IL-7 levels in VL patients are primarily due to increased production by CD4+ T cells, which contributes to the observed upregulation of IL-7Rα and enhanced signaling capacity.
439.Discussion_Source: PBMC and CD4+ cells are not a major source of IL-7, and analysis of IL7 mRNA expression did not show any differences between IL7 mRNA between VL and EC cells (Fig 2A). Surprisingly, analysis of soluble IL-7 in plasma demonstrated that the IL-7 levels were significantly higher in active, VL compared to EC (p<0.001) (Fig 2B).
Evidence: PBMC and CD4+ cells are not a major source of IL-7, and analysis of IL7 mRNA expression did not show any differences between IL7 mRNA between VL and EC cells (Fig 2A).
Increased plasma level of IL-7 is a sign of lymphopenia [30,31], something frequently observed in VL patients [32].
The increased IL-7 signaling may be a response to the lymphopenia to support the survival of existing T cells in VL.
439.Rationale: This distractor misattributes the source of elevated IL-7 to CD4+ T cells, which the passage explicitly states are not a major source. The passage notes that IL-7 is produced by nonhematopoietic cells and that no difference in IL7 mRNA was found in CD4+ T cells. The elevated IL-7 is instead interpreted as a consequence of lymphopenia, not a result of T cell production, making this inference unsupported.

439.Option: The higher pSTAT5 levels in activated CD4+ T cells of VL patients indicate that IL-7 signaling is fully functional and sufficient to drive effective immune responses during visceral leishmaniasis.
439.Discussion_Source: When comparing pSTAT5 in CD38+ compared to CD38- CD4+ T cells, less pSTAT5 was seen in the CD38+ CD4+ T cell subset from VL patients, where the pSTAT5 levels were similar in the two subsets in ECs, suggesting that there is a feedback mechanism to downregulate IL-7 signaling upon activation.
Evidence: The increased IL-7 signaling may be a response to the lymphopenia to support the survival of existing T cells in VL.

With elevated expression and secretion of Fas/FasL [41] and IFN-γ [42] being a reported features in VL patients, the high IL-7 levels and the elevated IL-7 signaling assumed to promote cell survival and proliferation could potentially simultaneously prime the T cell for Fas/FasL induced death.
Our data does not support impaired IL-7 signaling as an explanation for loss of CD4+ T cells during VL.
439.Rationale: This distractor reverses the interpretation of the data by assuming that high pSTAT5 in activated cells indicates functional efficacy. However, the passage shows that pSTAT5 is actually lower in activated (CD38+) cells, suggesting a regulatory feedback mechanism. The correct inference is that despite high signaling capacity, activation leads to downregulation, which may contribute to dysfunction. This distractor ignores the key finding about differential signaling in activated vs. non-activated subsets.

440.Option: The high cost of managing inpatient cases of malaria in pregnancy is primarily driven by the need for prolonged hospital stays and intensive care, rather than by the species of Plasmodium involved.
440.Discussion_Source: The cost of one bed/day was US$ 118.51 (ranging from US$ 111.64 to US$ 124.57) and considering the whole length of stay the cost of management of an inpatient case was US$ 355.53 (ranging from US$ 334.92 to US$ 373.71).
Evidence: All the 15 malaria admissions were due to P. vivax.
The cost of managing an outpatient case (multiple consultations) was US$ 103.51 for a P. vivax malaria episode and US$ 83.59 for a P. falciparum malaria episode.
Total provider costs for all malaria cases in pregnant women reported in Manaus in 2010 were US$ 17,038.50, of which US$ 15,741.14 (92.4%) was due to P. vivax malaria.
All women experiencing two episodes during the study period had P. vivax malaria.
440.Rationale: This distractor incorrectly attributes the high cost of inpatient care to the length of stay and intensive care, ignoring the passage's evidence that P. vivax is the dominant species in admissions and that repeated episodes—almost exclusively P. vivax—lead to significantly higher cumulative costs. The passage does not provide data on the intensity of care or ICU use, and the inference that species is irrelevant to cost is contradicted by the disproportionate share of total costs attributed to P. vivax despite its lower per-episode cost in outpatient settings.

440.Option: The economic burden of P. falciparum malaria in pregnancy is underestimated in this study due to its lower prevalence and the fact that it is more likely to be misdiagnosed as P. vivax.
440.Discussion_Source: The study reveals that the cost of treating malaria in pregnancy underwent a remarkable increase particularly when the patients required admission and in terms of indirect cost, and when the cost of subsequent P. vivax malaria episodes were considered.
Evidence: Of the 73 episodes, 96% (70/73) were due to P. vivax and the remaining 4% (3/73) due to P. falciparum.
Total provider costs for all malaria cases in pregnant women reported in Manaus in 2010 were US$ 17,038.50, of which US$ 15,741.14 (92.4%) was due to P. vivax malaria.
The cost of managing an outpatient case was US$ 83.59 for a P. falciparum malaria episode.
All women experiencing two episodes during the study period had P. vivax malaria.
440.Rationale: This distractor introduces an unverified claim about misdiagnosis and underestimation of P. falciparum burden, which is not supported by the passage. The passage states that all cases were microscopically confirmed and that P. falciparum cases were rare. There is no evidence of diagnostic error or underreporting of P. falciparum. The inference that P. falciparum is underrepresented due to misdiagnosis is speculative and not derivable from the data provided.

440.Option: The use of preventive measures such as bed nets and insecticides is associated with lower treatment costs for P. vivax malaria in pregnant women, suggesting that prevention is cost-effective.
440.Discussion_Source: Study women reported low use and expenses on preventive tools. Insecticide-treated nets (ITNs) have recently been introduced as a malaria control tool in the area and distributed for free to the population. Therefore, at the time of the study, the few pregnant women reporting bed net use were very likely to be using a non-insecticide treated net instead of ITNs. Median cost on preventive tools among women who were admitted were lower than among those who were treated as outpatients, suggesting that lower expenditure in malaria prevention may be associated with a higher risk of severe disease.
Evidence: The reported use of bed nets by study women was low (6%; 4/73) and 12% (9/73) reported having taken weekly chloroquine as a prevention of malaria relapse.
Median cost on preventive tools among women who were admitted were lower than among those who were treated as outpatients.
All women experiencing two episodes during the study period had P. vivax malaria.
The cost of managing an outpatient case was US$ 103.51 for a P. vivax malaria episode.
440.Rationale: This distractor assumes a causal relationship between preventive tool use and lower treatment costs, which is not supported by the passage. The study only notes that preventive tool use was low and that women with higher costs (e.g., admissions) had lower preventive spending. However, no data link preventive use directly to cost reduction. The inference that prevention is cost-effective is speculative and not derived from the clinical or economic data presented.

440.Option: The higher total provider costs for P. vivax malaria are due to the greater number of outpatient consultations required for its treatment compared to P. falciparum.
440.Discussion_Source: The cost of managing an outpatient case (multiple consultations) was US$ 103.51 (range US$ 92.93 to US$ 112.65) for a P. vivax malaria episode and US$ 83.59 (range US$ 76.15 to US$ 90.07) for a P. falciparum malaria episode.
Evidence: The cost of managing an outpatient case was higher for P. vivax than for P. falciparum.
Total provider costs for all malaria cases in pregnant women reported in Manaus in 2010 were US$ 17,038.50, of which US$ 15,741.14 (92.4%) was due to P. vivax malaria.
All women experiencing two episodes during the study period had P. vivax malaria.
The cost of one bed/day was US$ 118.51 and the cost of an inpatient case was US$ 355.53.
440.Rationale: This distractor misattributes the higher total costs of P. vivax to the number of outpatient consultations, but the passage does not provide data on the number of consultations per episode. The higher cost per episode for P. vivax is already accounted for in the unit cost, and the main driver of cumulative cost is the high rate of repeated episodes—almost exclusively P. vivax—leading to multiple treatments. The inference that more consultations cause higher costs is not supported by the data and reverses the actual causal chain: repeated infections, not consultation frequency, drive cost increases.

441.Option: The quinolone-based HDAC inhibitor NF2839 shows selective inhibition of SmHDAC8 over other HDAC isoforms, which explains its potent schistosomicidal activity and specific impact on egg maturation.
441.Discussion_Source: All three quinolone-based compounds inhibited the activity of the recombinant form of SmHDAC8 at the low micromolar range (Table 1). Therefore, these HDAC inhibitors have a deacetylase inhibitory activity in parasites and are also targeting SmHDAC8. The increase in tubulin acetylation in cell lysates of HDAC inhibitors-treated worm pairs (Fig 6) and their nanomolar inhibitory activity on hHDAC6 (Table 1) point to the possibility that others Class I or II HDACs could be targeted.
